entry.title: Managing Parent-Child Hierarchies in SAC: A Datasphere-Driven Approach
entry.content:
Introduction

Setting up Public Dimensions correctly is a core requirement for any successful SAC Planning project. While Seamless Planning already positions SAP Datasphere as the central source for fact data, the same does not yet apply for master data. I will share key lessons learned from real-world implementations and show how to leverage Datasphere to avoid SAC pitfalls - from data acquisition to visualization.

In this example, a new cost center dimension is created with a parent-child hierarchy. The hierarchy loads into SAC and displays correctly in the story. Standard features such as disaggregation work perfectly out of the box. Up to this point, everything appears straightforward.

SAC Hierarchy Management

 

Hierarchy in Story

 

Empty Nodes

Issues arise when hierarchies change over time or contain empty nodes. One challenge is that SAC automatically creates hierarchy nodes as new dimension members. In typical S/4HANA scenarios, these nodes are text nodes rather than actual master data members. SAC also does not request a node type, making it difficult to distinguish between real dimension members and text nodes.

In the example below, the hierarchy node “Marketing” is replaced by “Administration.” Since “Marketing” no longer exists in the source, it is not updated and remains in the SAC hierarchy. Because it no longer has children, SAC treats it as a leaf member and marks it as input-enabled. As a result, disaggregation may push values into a text node that no longer exists in the source system - an outcome that rarely makes sense:

Orphaned Node

A simple workaround for empty nodes - whether they are empty in the source or become empty because their children move - is to explicitly define node types using a dimension property.

By introducing a property such as NodeType, you can classify members as:

  • L = Leaf
  • N = Node
  • R = Root

If your source is S/4HANA, this information is available directly (at least for hierarchies created via "Manage Global Hierarchies" app). For other sources, the SAP HANA hierarchy developer guide provides useful SQLScript expressions to derive node types.

When applying a filter such as NodeType = "L" in the story, empty nodes are hidden - even with Show Unbooked enabled - and disaggregation no longer targets these nodes.

Input Sheet with Property Filter

 

Leaf Members Leaving the Hierarchy

Another challenge occurs when a hierarchy loses leaf members. In this example, the leaf member “M_C” is no longer part of the hierarchy. SAC only updates members that appear in the source. If “M_C” is missing from the source, it remains under its previous parent node (here: “Administration”), even though it should no longer belong there. The question is: how do we ensure that “M_C” correctly leaves the hierarchy?

With Seamless Planning and Datasphere, the SAC Public Dimension table can be exposed to a Datasphere space. Although hierarchy columns are not part of the exposed master data table, we can get the information about existing members in SAC:

Exposed in Datasphere

 

 

Once the current dimension members are available in Datasphere, the hierarchy load can be enhanced with a simple SQL statement that also updates members that are not present in the source. For these members, the SQL logic can clear the parent column, ensuring they correctly leave the hierarchy.

Unfortunately, the load mechanism sets members as root, when they are part of the data source and left blank in the parent node:

Blank becomes <root>

 

So, leaf member "M_C" actually doesn't leave the hierarchy, it's just not part of the "TOTAL" node anymore. 

 

Conclusion

SAC dimensions with hierarchies can be created quickly, but challenges arise when loading hierarchies via external sources:

  • text nodes without children
  • text nodes removed from the source
  • leaf members removed from the source

Each of these issues can be addressed without overly complex solutions when Datasphere with Seamless Planning is used as the middleware. However, the effort and potential for errors should not be underestimated.

Even when all recommended measures are implemented, two limitations remain: First, orphaned nodes cannot be deleted from an SAC Public Dimension through a load process. Because SAC does not allow external deletion of master data records, these members remain in the dimension as unused entries. Second, former leaf members cannot truly be removed from the hierarchy through the load process. Instead, they can only be converted into root members, which may lead to additional clean-up effort or modeling inconsistencies.

Overall, the SAC hierarchy concept works very well when hierarchies are built directly within SAC. However, loading and maintaining hierarchies from external systems quickly becomes complex. Certain aspects (such as hierarchy sorting) are still not sufficiently supported. Hopefully, future enhancements to Seamless Planning - especially using Datasphere views as sources for SAC Public Dimensions - will simplify hierarchy loadling and provide a clearer distinction between true dimension members and hierarchy metadata.

entry.title: Re: ECP connectivity with MBC (Mutli Bank Connectivity)
entry.content:
Hi all,

Just to add some clarification here.
SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (ECP) can be integrated with SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity, but it is not a plug‑and‑play scenario like SAP S/4HANA Cloud. The integration requires specific payroll add‑ons and a supported technical setup, and the exact components depend on the ECP release and deployment model.
There is no single public “end‑to‑end” guide comparable to the S/4HANA Cloud best practices. The recommended approach is:
- Ensure the required payroll add‑ons/support packages are in place on the ECP side
- Secure the SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity license
- Engage the SAP MBC onboarding team, who will validate the landscape and guide the supported integration approach
For this reason, detailed technical documentation is typically shared during onboarding rather than published as a generic Help Portal guide.
If you are proceeding, raising a formal MBC onboarding request is the best next step to get confirmation for your specific ECP setup.

Regards
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: Managing Parent-Child Hierarchies in SAC: A Datasphere-Driven Approach
entry.content: Great breakdown, @Tano_B! The issue of SAC treating removed nodes as input-enabled leaf members is a real headache for planning accuracy.Your workaround using a NodeType property (L, N, R) to filter the Story is a clever, low-complexity fix. It’s a great example of how Datasphere can act as the 'logic engine' to clean up SAC Public Dimensions when the source system falls short.Looking forward to future SAP updates that might finally allow for the external deletion of these orphaned records!

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud TRM-Special G/L indicator not define...
entry.content:
Hi @Marina_Blanco10,

This discrepancy between TBB1 and Process Business Transactions often points to a missing link in the Posting Specifications.

Since TBB1 is working, your account determination is likely fine, but the system may be struggling to identify the Special G/L indicator for the specific Update Type used in the intercompany flow. I'd recommend checking the following:

  • Assign Special G/L Indicator to Update Type: Verify if the specific Update Type (e.g., for principal or interest) is explicitly mapped to the Special G/L indicator in your configuration.

  • Customer/Vendor Integration: Since this is an intercompany loan, ensure the business partner is correctly linked to a Customer/Vendor role and that the reconciliation account supports the indicator you are trying to trigger.

  • Posting Key Logic: Ensure the posting key defined in your 'Indicate Account Determination' allows for Special G/L transactions.

I hope this helps you narrow down the issue!

entry.title: Re: S4 HANA to MBC configuration
entry.content:
Hi Dhana,

Your understanding is correct, and this is a common point of confusion when starting with SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity.

SAP Help Portal does provide “how‑to” documentation for MBC, but it is not published as a single step‑by‑step guide with fixed input values for each CBC activity. This is intentional.

The reason is that the MBC connector configuration in S/4HANA Cloud is connectivity‑specific and bank‑specific. The required CBC activities and their input values depend on:

  • The connectivity type (Member Bank, SFTP‑H2H, SWIFT, EBICS)
  • Whether the flow is inbound, outbound, or both
  • Bank‑specific agreements and routing requirements

In your scenario you are using two different connectivity types:

  • HSBC as a Member Bank
  • BSF via SFTP (Host‑to‑Host)

Because of this, not all CBC steps you listed are required. For example:

  • EBICS‑related activities apply only if EBICS is used
  • SWIFT parameters apply only for SWIFT connectivity
  • Routing and processing steps differ between inbound and outbound flows

SAP Help Portal explains:

  • What each CBC activity is used for
  • When it applies (which connectivity type)
  • Whether it is relevant for inbound or outbound processing

However, SAP does not publish sample values such as Sender IDs, Receiver IDs, or routing rules, because these are validated during SAP MBC onboarding and differ per customer and bank.

The recommended approach is:

  • Use SAP Help Portal to understand which CBC activities are relevant
  • Complete MBC onboarding for each connectivity type (Member Bank and SFTP)
  • Apply the exact input values and routing confirmed during onboarding

Helpful starting points:

  • SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity overview and onboarding guide
    https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud – Bank integration and CBC configuration
    https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD

In summary, the Help Portal provides the “how it works”, while the exact “what to enter” is confirmed as part of the MBC onboarding process. This is why there is no generic step‑by‑step guide with example values.

Hope this clarifies the approach and helps you proceed.

Kind regards
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: Bank Integration with SAP MBC
entry.content:
Hi Hong Wah,

That’s a good follow‑up question, and it’s important to clarify expectations here.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity cannot be used as a generic upload or staging area where payment files generated by a third‑party system are manually placed and then forwarded to the bank.

In the Public Cloud:

  • Outbound payment files must be generated by supported SAP payment processes in S/4HANA Cloud and then handed over to SAP MBC.
  • MBC does not provide a user option to manually upload a third‑party payment file and push it to the bank.
  • Files with unknown or unsupported message types can be received in MBC and viewed/downloaded via Manage Bank Messages, but they are not automatically processed as payments.

The scope item 16R (Bank Integration with SAP MBC) supports sending payment instructions that originate from SAP payment runs. Using MBC to forward externally generated payment files is not supported in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

If a third‑party system must generate payment files, this would typically require handling outside of S/4HANA Cloud (for example via middleware or bank portals), rather than using SAP MBC as the dispatch mechanism.

I hope this clarifies the supported usage.

Kind regards
Mahendra C
MBC  Team

entry.title: Re: Troubleshooting Made Easy: Dive into Cloud Connector’s Built-in Diagnostics
entry.content:
Great deep dive, @MarcoErtel! In my research on Smart Ports and Logistics 4.0, we often discuss the critical importance of low-latency, reliable data tunnels for real-time corridor optimization.

The 'passive' nature of this troubleshooting framework is a huge win for operational efficiency. The Average Recurrence Interval feature is particularly valuable—it helps distinguish between transient network 'noise' and systematic bottlenecks that could disrupt high-frequency RFC calls in a synchronized supply chain.

For anyone integrating SAP EWM/TM in a hybrid landscape, making this page a 30-second daily pitstop is a solid recommendation to ensure seamless connectivity before an issue impacts the warehouse floor. Thanks for sharing!

entry.title: Re: Latest people profile compa ration cannot be hidden
entry.content:
Hi @VM90,

This sounds like a known limitation with the Latest People Profile (LPP) rendering engine. While Legacy profile respected conditional visibility via business rules across all views, the LPP 'View All' screen often defaults to the Data Model permissions or RBPs rather than dynamic UI rules.

A few things to verify for a potential workaround:

  • Rule Trigger: Ensure your rule is also assigned as an onView trigger on the compInfo element in the Succession Data Model or via 'Manage Business Configuration' (BCUI). LPP relies heavily on onView for initial page loads.

  • Field-Level Permissions: If the condition is based on a static attribute (like Country or Legal Entity), try using Role-Based Permissions (RBP) instead of a business rule. LPP handles RBP-based hiding much more consistently on the 'View All' screen.

  • MDF vs. Legacy EC: If you are using an MDF-based block, check the 'Visibility' attribute in the Object Definition. For standard compInfo, verify if the field is set to 'View' in the Data Model.

If the rule works in Edit and History but fails specifically on 'View All', it might be worth checking the Known Issues for Latest People Profile in the SAP Support Portal, as many UI-rule behaviors are still being patched to match Legacy parity.

entry.title: Re: MBC and Norma43 file format
entry.content:
Hi Ariana,

It’s important to distinguish between transport and processing, as this is where the confusion usually comes from.

SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity is format‑agnostic from a transport perspective. This means MBC can technically carry any file format (including Norma 43) without changing or interpreting the payload.

However, in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, only certain bank statement formats are supported for automatic processing and posting (for example CAMT.053 or MT940). Norma 43 is not a supported bank statement processing format in S/4HANA Cloud.

So in practice:

  • ✅ MBC can transport a Norma 43 file
  • ❌ S/4HANA Cloud cannot automatically interpret or post a Norma 43 file
  • ✅ Spanish banks typically need to provide CAMT.053 or MT940 for direct integration with S/4HANA Cloud
  • Alternatively, Norma 43 would need to be converted externally before being consumed by SAP

In summary, MBC does not limit the file format, but S/4HANA Cloud supports only specific formats for bank statement processing, which is why Norma 43 is not used directly in standard S/4HANA Cloud integrations.

Hope this clears up the distinction.

Kind regards
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: SmartRecruiters and SF Recruiting coexistence – Creat...
entry.content:
Hi @barbara_gentner8286fb,

This is a classic 'Parallel Run' challenge. In standard SAP SuccessFactors Position Management, the 'Create Requisition' action from the Org Chart is typically hardcoded to trigger a specific integration flow or the internal SF Recruiting module.

To achieve a transition routed by population or position, you generally have two paths:

  • Business Rules Logic: You can potentially configure a Business Rule on the Position object that evaluates a specific attribute (e.g., 'Recruiting System' field or 'Legal Entity'). However, the standard 'Create Job' button in the Org Chart usually triggers a single destination based on your global Provisioning settings.

  • Intelligent Services Center (ISC): A more robust approach during a transition phase is to use ISC. When a 'Position Change' or 'Job Requisition' event is triggered, you can use the Integration Center to intercept the event and, based on logic (e.g., if Department = X), route the payload to SmartRecruiters via API or allow it to proceed to SF Recruiting.

  • Position-Level Attribute: Adding a custom field to the Position (e.g., cust_RecruitingPlatform) allows you to explicitly define the target system. This gives you the granular 'position-level' control you are looking for, though it requires manual maintenance during the transition.

In my experience with system integrations, the 'Target System' isn't globally forced if you leverage Business Rules to hide/show the 'Create Requisition' action or use middleware to handle the routing logic. I'd recommend checking if your SmartRecruiters integration is using the OData API for requisition creation, as that's where your conditional routing logic will be most effective.

entry.title: Re: MBC Connector Communication
entry.content:
Hi Edgardo,

This is a shared responsibility.
SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity implementation typically involves multiple roles:
- Functional consultants: payment setup, bank integration processes, and Payment Medium Workbench (PMW) configuration
- Basis / technical team: SSL/TLS certificates, connectivity, secure communication, and system prerequisites
- ABAP team (if needed): enhancements or BADIs, for example when Sender ID cannot be derived via standard House Bank configuration

In addition, the SAP MBC onboarding team supports the setup on the MBC tenant side (bank connectivity, routing, security validation).
So it’s usually a joint effort between functional, technical/Basis, and ABAP teams, coordinated with SAP MBC onboarding.

Regards
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: How do you design a scalable POS architecture integra...
entry.content:
Hi @Suheb,

Designing for scalability in a POS-S/4HANA landscape requires a balance between local autonomy and 'Clean Core' governance. From my perspective in Industrial Systems Engineering and Smart Logistics, I’d recommend a three-pillar approach:

  • Asynchronous Event-Driven Architecture: Don't let your POS wait for S/4HANA for every transaction. Use a middleware like SAP Integration Suite to queue sales data. This ensures the store stays operational even during network 'noise' or SAP maintenance windows.

  • Edge-to-Cloud Connectivity: Use the SAP Cloud Connector to manage your tunnels securely. A pro-tip for scalability is to monitor your RFC calls via the built-in Troubleshooting framework. By tracking the Average Recurrence Interval of connection timeouts, you can proactively identify which store locations have unstable hardware before it impacts peak shopping hours.

  • Master Data Integrity via Datasphere: To scale to multiple locations, treat your store and product hierarchies as Public Dimensions. I’ve found that using SAP Datasphere as a middleware helps manage complex parent-child hierarchies, allowing you to hide empty nodes or handle orphaned data without over-complicating the S/4HANA core.

By offloading the heavy transformation logic to the middleware, you keep S/4HANA 'clean' and ensure your POS system can grow as fast as your business footprint.

Hope this helps with your design!

entry.title: Re: Parallel Deletion In RAP managed Application
entry.content:
Hi @ULGIA,

This is an interesting edge case that highlights how the Draft framework changes the standard OData V4 orchestration in RAP.

The core issue here is likely how the OData V4 inbound handler processes the request before it even reaches your business logic. When with draft is active, the framework expects a specific state-handling sequence. If the record is already gone from the persistent table, the framework triggers an automatic HTTP 404 (Not Found) because the resource identified by the URL/Key no longer exists.

In a non-draft scenario (like your colleague's), the flow is more direct, allowing the precheck_delete to serve as the primary gatekeeper and return a 202 with your custom message.

To answer your specific points:

  • Is this a known limitation? It is less of a limitation and more of a standard OData V4 behavior where resource existence is verified before logic execution. With Draft enabled, this check is often stricter.

  • Intercepting the 404: Since the framework throws the 404 at the gateway level, it is very difficult to surface a custom backend message once that status is set.

  • The 'Draft' Decision: If your application is a List Report without an Object Page and editing is disabled, you are essentially using Draft for a read-only/delete-only scenario. In this case, removing with draft is likely the most efficient solution. It simplifies the state handling and allows your precheck_delete logic to function as intended, providing a better UX with your custom error message.

Unless you plan to implement complex 'Draft' features later, keeping the architecture 'Clean' by removing the unnecessary draft overhead would be my recommendation for this optimization.

entry.title: Re: MBC(16R) requires any additional license in india?
entry.content:
Hi Goutham,

To clarify both points—availability and licensing:

  • Licensing: SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity (scope item 16R) always requires a separate SAP MBC subscription, regardless of country. The scope item itself is part of S/4HANA Cloud best practices, but using MBC requires a paid MBC service subscription.

  • Availability in India: Earlier releases did not show 16R as available for India, which caused confusion. This has since changed—16R is now available for India in SAP S/4HANA Cloud. However, the licensing requirement remains unchanged.

So in summary:
✅ 16R is available for India
✅ A separate SAP MBC subscription is still required

Kind regards,
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: Manage Automatic Payments (via MBC) Email notify bank.
entry.content:
Hi,

Your understanding is broadly correct. SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity acts as a secure transmission and routing layer and, by design, does not interpret or open the payment file contents. As a result:

  • MBC cannot derive totals, number of payments, or other summary information from the file
  • MBC does not provide functionality to generate or send email notifications based on payment file content

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, there is also no standard event or BAdI that allows intercepting the final MBC payment file to read its content, change the file name, and trigger an email, while still letting MBC transmit the same file to the bank.

If a bank mandates an additional email summary (file name, totals, number of payments), the commonly used approaches are:

  • Side‑by‑side extension, for example using SAP Integration Suite or a custom middleware, where the payment file (or payment data) is processed separately to generate and send the email
  • Bank‑side handling, where the bank generates the confirmation email after receiving the file
  • Operational workaround, where the summary is produced from SAP payment run data (before transmission), outside of MBC

In short, this requirement is outside the standard scope of SAP MBC, and any solution would need to be implemented outside of MBC itself.

Hope this clarifies the limitations and possible options.

Kind regards
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: Zero Amount Invoice in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Editi...
entry.content:
Hi @LDB,

This is indeed standard behavior. The Manage Project Billing app (and the underlying billing plan engine in Professional Services) is designed to trigger revenue-bearing transactions, so it inherently blocks zero-value items to prevent 'empty' billing documents from flooding the accounting queue.

For a business case like a donation of IT devices where you need a zero-sum invoice but still want to maintain the link to the WBS element, here are three common approaches:

  • The 'Fixed Price' Workaround: Create a Fixed Price billing item for a nominal amount (e.g., 0.01 EUR) and then apply a 100% Header Discount or a Manual Price reduction to bring it to zero. Sometimes the framework accepts a zero-sum total if it originated from a non-zero base, though this depends on your specific item category configuration.

  • Manual Billing Document (VF01): If the Manage Project Billing app continues to block you, you can manually create a billing document of type 'External Billing' or a standard invoice (F2) and manually assign the WBS element at the item level. This bypasses the Project Billing engine while ensuring the costs/statistical data still reflect against the project in S/4HANA.

  • Sales Order Integration: Instead of using the Professional Services billing engine, create a standard Sales Order (VA01) for the donation. You can set the item category to be non-billing relevant or use a 100% discount, and crucially, you can link each line item to your project's WBS element. This provides a cleaner 'Proof of Delivery' for the 15 devices while staying integrated with your project reporting.

In my experience with Industrial Systems, using a dedicated Sales Order is often the 'cleaner' architectural choice for asset movements that don't follow the standard time-and-expenses or fixed-price revenue cycles. It keeps your Manage Project Billing view restricted to actual revenue, preventing clutter.

entry.title: Re: Manage Billing Documents:FAZ prepayment invoice vouch...
SAP Customer Conversations LIVE. Реальний досвід та результати
entry.content:
SAP Customer Conversations LIVE — формат, у якому референс-клієнт SAP представляє кейс використання рішення, ключові моменти впровадження та досягнуті бізнес-результати — після чого відбувається жива дискусія та інтерактивна Q&A сесія. Саме такий клієнтський досвід прискорює угоди та будує довіру.

Ваші клієнти матимуть змогу познайомитися з реальними кейсами трансформації, поспілкуватися наживо з колегами по галузі та отримати відповіді на запитання щодо ризиків і строків окупності. Для вас це означає довіру клієнта з першого кроку, переконливі аргументи проти будь-яких заперечень, коротші цикли продажів і більше виграних угод.

entry.title: Re: For the PR Header text Mandatory when PR select prior...
entry.content:
Hi @Joy14,

Since standard configuration doesn't support conditional text mandatory status, the best approach for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is to use the Cloud BAdIs available in the 'Custom Logic' app.

For Purchase Requisitions (PR), you should look into the following BAdI:

  • BAdI: MM_PUR_S4_PR_CHECK (Check of Purchase Requisition) This BAdI is triggered during the 'Check' and 'Save' actions. You can implement logic to:

    1. Read the value of your Custom Field for Priority.

    2. Check if the Header Text table is empty.

    3. If Priority = 'High' and Header Text is initial, populate the FAILED parameter and send a targeted error message to the REPORTED parameter.

Key Technical Tip: In S/4HANA Cloud, texts are often handled in a separate buffer. Ensure your logic checks the PURCHASEREQUISITIONTEXT entity or the relevant text collection within the BAdI interface.

If you prefer a more 'No-Code' approach, verify if the 'Manage Business Rules' for Sourcing and Procurement can handle this, though for specific text-existence checks, the ABAP Cloud BAdI mentioned above is generally the most reliable for 'Clean Core' compliance.

Hope this helps you secure your high-priority PR process!

entry.title: Re: Secondary phase not confirmed with planned activities...
entry.content:
Got Answer from SAP
Confirming planned values on secondary resources during milestone phase confirmation is not directly supported. The standard system provides the following confirmation capabilities:
Primary Phase Confirmation: When confirming a milestone phase, the system automatically confirms all preceding phases based on established relationships;
Secondary Resource Confirmation: Secondary resources must be confirmed separately using time ticket confirmation or time event confirmation processes;
Milestone Logic: Milestone confirmation triggers automatic confirmation of related phases but does not extend to secondary resource planned values.

entry.title: Re: What is the required checklist that SAP needs for MBC...
entry.content:
Hi Mike,

Yes, there is an official checklist, but it’s not openly published as a downloadable template on SAP Community or Help Portal.

For SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity, SAP uses a standard onboarding questionnaire / bank connectivity form, which is provided by the SAP MBC onboarding team once onboarding is initiated (via service request).

Typically, SAP requests the following information as part of that checklist:

  • SAP system details

    • S/4HANA Cloud tenant (Test / Production)
    • System IDs and geographical region

  • Bank details

    • Bank name(s)
    • Country
    • BIC / SWIFT codes (if applicable)

  • Connectivity type

    • Member Bank, SFTP (Host‑to‑Host), EBICS, or SWIFT

  • Message and file types

    • Payment formats (for example PAIN.001)
    • Status messages (for example PAIN.002)
    • Bank statements (CAMT, MT formats, or local formats)

  • Security and access

    • Certificates / keys (as applicable to the connectivity type)
    • SFTP details (folders, authentication method), if relevant

  • Contact and project details

    • Technical contacts
    • Project timeline and test windows

The onboarding questionnaire is typically shared:

  • After raising an MBC onboarding service request
  • Or via the assigned Technical Integration Engineer (TIE)

SAP Help Portal provides the process overview, but not the filled‑in checklist template:

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

So the recommended next step is to raise an MBC onboarding request, after which SAP will provide the official checklist tailored to your banks and connectivity type.

Hope this clarifies how the checklist is handled.

Kind regards
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: Data Collection Without Cart- in Webextend
entry.content:
Hi @MartonImrek,

A 33% error rate for 'Data-collection events without cart' usually suggests that the cart command is either missing from specific page templates or is being triggered in the wrong sequence during the page load.

In my experience with Industrial Systems and data flow optimization, empty events with only a timestamp typically point to one of these three technical scenarios:

  • Phantom Tracking on SPA/AJAX transitions: If your site is a Single Page Application, WebExtend might be triggering a 'page view' event during a route change before the cart object has been re-initialized. This results in the 'blank' events you see in your screenshot.

  • Race Conditions with Consent Managers: If the WebExtend scripts are waiting for a cookie consent banner, they might be firing a generic initialization event as soon as consent is given, but failing to pull the actual cart data in that initial pulse.

  • Missing cart command on non-commerce pages: Emarsys expects the cart command on every page to maintain the session state. If your agency only implemented it on the product and checkout pages, visiting a 'Blog' or 'About Us' page will trigger a data collection event that the system flags as 'without cart.'

I would recommend your agency uses the Emarsys Inspector (Chrome Extension) to browse those 'blank' pages. Check if the ScarabQueue.push(['cart', [...]]); is actually present in the console logs on every single URL. If the queue is empty, Predict loses the session context, leading to exactly what you've observed.

entry.title: Transporting SAP SuccessFactors Integrations via Configuration Transport Center (CTC)
entry.content:
1. Introduction

The purpose of this document is to outline the secure process of pairing two SuccessFactors instances and transporting Integration Center Definitions using the Configuration Transport Center (CTC). This method ensures consistency between Development/Preview and Production environments.

2. Phase 1: Instance Pairing (Prerequisite)

Before any transport can occur, a secure "handshake" must be established between the source and target systems.

2.1 Generate Pairing Token (Source Instance)

  1. Navigate to Admin Center > Configuration Transport Center > Instance Pair Wizard
  2. Click + Add to create a new pair.
  3. Enter the Target Data Center, Target Company ID, and Environment Type (e.g., Production).
  4. Click Generate Token. Copy and save this token immediately, as it will not be shown again (At Target).
  5. At Source(Test SF)

    

8. At Target (Prod SF) > Copy Token

     

9. At Source (Test SF) > Add Token > Validate

      

2.2 Authorize Pairing (Target Instance)

  1. Log into the Target Instance.
  2. Navigate to Admin Center > Instance Pairing Tool.
  3. Go to the Received Tokens tab (or follow the prompt to add the token).
  4. Paste the token and click Approve. The status should now show as Paired.

3. Phase 2: Create & Populate Transport Bundle

This step identifies the specific integration definitions to be moved.

3.1 Add to Bundle

  1. From the search results, check the boxes for the integrations you wish to transport.
  2. Click Add to Bundle.
  3. Select Create New Bundle.
  4. Bundle Name: Use a clear naming convention (e.g., Test_Prod_Integration).
  5. Change Request ID: Enter your internal ticket/tracking number.
  6. Click Create.

        

       

     

3.2 Search for Definitions

  1. Log into the Source Instance.
  2. Go to Admin Center > Configuration Transport Center.
  3. Select the Configuration Activities tab.
  4. Set Configuration Area to Platform.
  5. Set Configuration Type to Integration Center Definition.
  6. Click Go.

     

4. Phase 3: Initiate Transport

  1. Switch to the Bundle Management tab within the CTC.
  2. Locate your bundle and click the Actions (three dots) icon > Initiate Transport.
  3. Target Details: Select the paired Target Instance from the dropdown.

        

     4.  Review: Confirm the list of objects and click Transport. 

 

      

5. Phase 4: Validation & Import (Target Instance)

  1. Log into the Target Instance.
  2. Go to Configuration Transport Center > Transport Requests > Imports.
  3. Validate: Find your bundle and click Actions > Validate.
    • If Validation Fails: Check the logs for missing dependencies (e.g., MDF objects or Picklists).
  4. Import: Once the status is Validated, click Actions > Import.

         
          *Error due to no data present at Production yet

5. Result (At Target)         

       

6. Conclusion

This article provides an overall idea about the Transport Center in SuccessFactors and how to use it for transport of Integration definitions. Below is the overall process diagram of the above:

entry.title: CPI Package Design – How do you structure packages for cross-domain integrations?
entry.content:
Dear All,

I’d like to get some expert opinions on package structuring in SAP Integration Suite (CPI).

Currently, we have a package named “Procurement” that contains all integrations related to our Source-to-Pay platform such as:

  • Vendor master integration

  • Purchase order creation/updates

  • Invoice integration

  • Contract integration

These iFlows are all related to the procurement domain, so grouping them together made sense.

Now we have a new requirement:
Pull contract documents from OpenText ECM, process them in CPI, and place them into Azure Blob Storage for an AI Contract Analytics platform.

Flow:

OpenText ECM → CPI → Azure Blob → AI Contract Analytics

This integration is not directly related to Procurement, although the documents are procurement contracts.

So the question is:

Would you place this integration in the existing “Procurement” package, or create a new package (e.g., “OpenText-Contract-Integration” or “OpenText Integration”)?

I’m interested in hearing how others structure CPI packages in large landscapes. Do you typically organize by:

• Business domain (Procurement, Finance, HR)
• System pair (SAP–Ariba, SAP–OpenText, etc.)
• Capability/domain (Document Management, Identity, etc.)

Additionally, if a package becomes too large or contains many iFlows, do you recommend splitting it into smaller packages, and if so, what criteria do you usually follow?

Looking forward to hearing how other CPI architects approach this.

entry.title: Re: Control execution of subsequent actions based on Labo...
entry.content:
Hi @okakoshow,

In SAP Digital Manufacturing POD 1.0, the standard action list on a button follows a linear execution model that doesn't inherently support conditional 'interrupts' from one plugin to the next.

To achieve this level of control and maintain system integrity, the 'Clean Core' approach is to move the logic from the POD button into the Production Process Designer. Instead of two actions on the button, assign a single Production Process that acts as an orchestrator:

  • Orchestration Flow: Within the process, call the Labor On service.

  • Conditional Branching: Use a Decision Task immediately after the Labor On call to evaluate the service response.

  • Validation: If the badge is invalid or the dialog is cancelled (returned as a failed status), the process can trigger an End Event or a Message Plugin, effectively blocking Production Process A from ever starting.

This pattern is much more robust for industrial environments because it ensures that machine-level execution (Process A) is strictly dependent on a verified 'Labor On' state.

If you need the specific 'Dialog' experience of the Labor On plugin, you might also consider a Custom UI5 Plugin that wraps the API calls, but for most standard implementations, service orchestration in the Designer is the recommended path.

Hope this helps you secure your shop floor workflow!

entry.title: Re: Issue in Business Process regarding BP
entry.content:
Hi @Mpaakhawa,

This is a classic CVI (Customer Vendor Integration) synchronization issue. While S/4HANA uses BP as the entry point, transactions like F-47 and ME21N still look for the underlying Vendor Master data.

If the system only allows the Vendor number, it means your Number Grouping is likely set to different ranges for BP and Vendor. To use the BP number in these fields, you must ensure:

  1. The BP is extended to roles FLVN00 and FLVN01.

  2. In the CVI configuration, the 'Same Number' indicator is checked for the BP-to-Vendor mapping.

Check your settings in SPRO under Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization. This should allow you to unify the numbers and streamline your procurement process!

entry.title: Re: There's a tutorial where I don't know how to proceed.
entry.content:
Hi @Yusuke20250417,

This is a common hurdle when transitioning to the Three-System Landscape (3SL) in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. The behavior you're seeing is actually by design to maintain a strict separation between Business Configuration and Developer Extensibility.

To proceed with the tutorial in a TDD environment, you need to understand the roles of your two tenants:

  • Customizing Tenant (100): Think of this as your 'Operational' hub. You cannot use ADTs/Eclipse here because it’s meant for standard configurations. You must create your Communication Arrangement for SAP_COM_0008 here to technically expose the service.

  • Development Tenant (080): This is your 'Sandbox' for ABAP Cloud. While you can’t select standard arrangements like 0008 here, you don’t actually need to. You only need the Metadata ($metadata) from the service you exposed in Tenant 100 to generate your Service Consumption Model in Eclipse.

The 'Fix' for your Tutorial: When you configure the BTP Destination, point it to the Customizing Tenant (100) URL. This is where the 'Communication Arrangement' lives and where the authentication will be validated. You only use the Development Tenant (080) to write the wrapper logic if you are building a custom outbound service.

From an Industrial Systems perspective, this separation ensures a 'Clean Core'—keeping standard API exposures (Tenant 100) distinct from custom code developments (Tenant 080).

Hope this helps you bridge the gap between the two tenants!

entry.title: Re: End to end scenario of Swift integration using proxie...
entry.content:
Hi Rajani,

This is a good question, and it’s important to clarify the architecture first.

From an SAP standard and recommended perspective, SAP PI / PO with the SWIFT integration package and SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity (MBC) are not designed to be used together in the same end‑to‑end SWIFT payment flow.

Here’s the key distinction:

  • SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity (MBC) already provides embedded SWIFT connectivity (FIN and FileAct), operated and managed by SAP.
  • When using MBC, SAP S/4HANA connects directly to MBC via the MBC connector, and MBC handles the SWIFT network communication.
  • In this scenario, SAP PI proxies or the SAP PI SWIFT package are not required.

If you build a flow like:

S/4 → PI (SWIFT proxies) → SWIFT → Bank

then MBC is not used.

If you use:

S/4 → MBC → SWIFT → Bank

then PI is not part of the SWIFT integration.

Because of this, SAP does not provide an official “end‑to‑end” scenario or blog that combines:

  • SAP PI SWIFT proxies and
  • SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity

The SAP‑recommended approaches today are:

  • Use MBC for SWIFT connectivity (preferred, SAP‑managed, cloud‑ready)
  •  Or use SAP PI / PO SWIFT integration as an alternative, without MBC
  •  Do not mix both for the same SWIFT payment flow

Useful references:

  • Corporations Using SWIFT via SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity (SAP Help Portal)
  • SWIFT Message Types: FIN and FileAct (SAP Help Portal)
  • Connector for SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity – Configuration Guide

If your requirement is to modernize an existing PI‑based SWIFT setup, SAP generally recommends moving to MBC instead of combining both architectures.

Hope this clarifies the design options.

Kind regards
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: MBC- EBS : different company Id's for different accou...
entry.content:
Hi Amit,

This requirement is understandable, but there is an important limitation to be aware of in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with SAP MBC:

  • The Company ID / Sender ID is derived at House Bank level, not at individual bank account level.
  • When multiple bank accounts (EUR, GBP, USD, etc.) exist under the same house bank, only one Sender ID can be maintained for that house bank.
  • It is not supported to maintain different Sender IDs for different bank accounts within the same house bank in standard configuration.

Implications for your scenario:

  • You cannot create a second Company ID / Sender ID specifically for the EUR account while keeping the same house bank.
  • The DME / PMW formats (for example GB_CGI_XML_CT) will always use the Sender ID derived from the House Bank data.
  • There is no supported way to map Sender ID at the bank‑account level in either MBC or DME configuration.

Supported alternatives (if business requires different IDs):

  1. Create separate House Banks
    Maintain EUR as a separate house bank, even if the physical bank is the same. This allows assigning a different Customer Number (Sender ID).
  2. Align with bank to accept a single Sender ID
    This is the most common approach in multi‑currency setups.
  3. Custom enhancement (not recommended in Public Cloud)
    Sender ID manipulation via BAdI is technically limited and not considered a supported or upgrade‑safe option in S/4HANA Cloud.

 In short:

Different Sender IDs per bank account within the same house bank are not supported in S/4HANA Cloud + MBC.

If the bank mandates different IDs per currency, the only standard, supported solution is to split the house bank structure.

Kind regards
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: Depreciation Start Date Requirement – Beginning of Fi...
entry.content:
Hi @parasyadavgalana,

Yes, this is possible in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition using standard Asset Accounting configuration.

The depreciation start date is driven by the Period Control assigned to the acquisition. To meet your requirement, you should use the standard Period Control 06 (At the start of the year).

Steps to implement:

  1. Verify or create a Period Control Method that assigns value 06 to the 'Acquisition' transaction type.

  2. Update your Depreciation Key to use this Period Control Method.

  3. When you capitalize an asset (e.g., on 15-Nov-2026), the system will automatically derive the Depreciation Start Date as 01-Jan-2026, assuming a standard calendar fiscal year.

From an Industrial Systems perspective, this 'catch-up' depreciation approach is common for specific tax or regulatory reporting, but I'd recommend testing it first in your Q-tenant to ensure the first-year depreciation catch-up posting aligns with your accounting expectations.

entry.title: Purchase Card Integration with SAP Concur
entry.content:
Introduction: SAP Concur seamlessly integrates purchasing cards (P‑cards) with expense management by automatically importing and categorizing transactions. This automation simplifies expense review, reconciliation, and approval while enhancing visibility into spending trends for better data analysis and vendor negotiations. The system enforces policy rules, manages workflows, and aligns invoices with pre-approved purchases to ensure accuracy and compliance.

Follow the below steps to integrate Purchase Card with SAP Concur:

Step-1: Create the Purchase Card Policy

Navigate to Administration > Expense > Policies. Select any existing policy and click on “Copy Policy” to create new policy

Fill in all the required details to configure the policy. Review all the information entered and click on “Save” to create the Purchase card Policy.

Step 2: Configure Purchase Card Feed Import Definition

  • To integrate purchase card with SAP Concur, a Partner case must be raised with Concur for the feed setup.
  • Once the purchase card integration setup is completed, all purchase cards will be mapped automatically to the employees.

Step 3: Review Purchase Cards mapping to the Card Program

Ensure each purchase card account is correctly linked to its corresponding card program. Proper association enables accurate expense tracking, smoother reconciliation, and more reliable reporting.

Only users with the Company Card Administrator role have permission to assign individual purchasing card accounts to a program.

  1. Navigate to Administration > Company > Tools > Company Card > Manage Accounts.
  2. Search for purchase card accounts by using the Search in options such as Name on Card, Employee Name, or Employee ID as same as corporate cards

     

 

 

      3. Select the desired row and choose Modify to open the Modify Card Account dialog box.

 

      4. Select the appropriate Card Program Name (required).

      5. Click Save.

Step 4: Review Merchant Codes to Expense Types Mappings

Ensure all merchant category codes (MCCs) are correctly mapped to expense types per Purchase Card policy for accurate classification and compliance.

To review and update merchant code mappings:

  1. Go to Administration > Company > Tools > Company Card > Merchant Code Mapping.

       2. Verify all MCCs are present and correctly labelled, mapped to the right expense types.

       3. For each expense type, check its status:

  • If mapped, select it, click Modify to verify or add MCCs via Map New.
  • If Not Mapped, select it, click Modify > Map New, map respective merchant code.       

       4. To configure MCC settings, select an expense type, click Modify, choose the MCC, and click Modify again.

       5. Review Expense Type & merchant code mappings and click “Next” to continue.

    6. Review the mappings, make any necessary adjustments, and then click Done.

Step 5: Validate Purchase Card Settings

Review the purchase card settings:

  1. Navigate to Administration > Company > Tools > Company Card.
  2. Select the Settings tab.

      3. Modify the settings accordingly.

       4. Click on “Save”

Benefits of Using Purchase Card Integration with SAP Concur

  1. Automatic Data Feed: P‑Card transactions are automatically imported from the bank into Concur, eliminating manual expense entry and reducing user errors.
  2. Real-Time Financial Insight: Finance teams get real‑time insight into purchasing activity across departments, vendors, and cost centers through dashboards and reports.
  3. Stronger Policy Compliance: Concur enforces expense rules (MCC restrictions, limits, receipt requirements) at transaction level, reducing non‑compliant spending.
  4. Reduced Invoice Processing Costs: P‑Cards minimize low‑value invoices and AP workload by shifting spend directly to card transactions.

General FAQs:

Q1. What is a Purchase Card (P-Card) in SAP Concur?

A:  A company-issued payment card for purchases, with transactions automatically imported into SAP Concur for employee review and processing.

Q2. Can employees delete P-Card transactions?

A:  No, transactions cannot be deleted but can be reconciled, flagged, commented on, or returned based on permissions.

Q3. What if an expense report is submitted without all P-Card transactions assigned?

A:  Concur may block submission or alert users; unassigned transactions remain in Available Expenses until reconciled.

Q4. How does SAP Concur ensure P-Card compliance?

A: By enforcing policies through rules, audits, receipt requirements, and approval workflows before reimbursement or reconciliation.

entry.title: Re: Getting Started with SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity
entry.content:
Hi,

Great question.

SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity does not provide a full customer‑specific demo tenant that partners can freely use for presales scenarios. MBC is a contract‑based, SAP‑managed service, and tenants (test/production) are provisioned only after a commercial agreement and onboarding are in place.

For presales and demo purposes, the commonly supported options are:

  • SAP Demo Store scenarios (high‑level functional demos showing MBC concepts and end‑to‑end flows)
  • Recorded demos, slide decks, and reference architectures provided via SAP sales enablement
  • Explain & show approach using SAP S/4HANA Cloud demo systems combined with MBC conceptual material

If the bank itself is the customer and wants to use MBC as part of daily operations, a formal MBC subscription and onboarding process is required. In such cases, SAP will provision the required MBC tenants (test and production) as part of the contract.

For presales support, it’s best to:

  • Coordinate with the SAP Account Executive
  • Use SAP Demo Store and official MBC enablement content
  • Position MBC using reference demos, not live transactional systems

Hope this clarifies the available options.

Kind regards
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: Integration C4C --> S4: How to block a contact person...
entry.content:
Hi @Christian77,

This is a classic 'Status vs. Relationship' mismatch. In S/4HANA, the sales order entry (VA01) looks at the BP Relationship (Table BUT050) to determine which contact persons are available.

The issue is that blocking the contact in C4C often only updates the individual BP record, but it doesn't always 'expire' the relationship.

Best Practice Setup:

  1. Map Status to Validity: Configure your integration (CPI/PI) so that a 'Blocked' or 'Obsolete' status in C4C maps to the Validity End Date of the relationship in S/4HANA.

  2. Expire the Relationship: By setting the Valid To date to today, the relationship becomes technically inactive. S/4HANA will then automatically hide this contact from the partner selection list in VA01.

  3. Filter in CVI: Ensure your Customer/Vendor Integration (CVI) settings are not set to ignore status updates for existing relationships.

In my experience with Industrial Systems, ensuring that the 'Validity' of the link is updated—rather than just the status of the person—is the only way to prevent these downstream replication errors.

Hope this helps you clean up your order replication flow!

entry.title: Re: Calling All EHS Professionals:
entry.content:
This sounds like a fantastic, high-impact format, @Jessica_Bartels!

From my research in Smart Ports and Logistics 4.0, I’ve seen how audit and inspection data often remain siloed, yet they are the 'digital bedrock' for implementing reliable AI-driven predictive maintenance. In complex maritime corridors, the transition from manual inspections to automated, AI-supported audits is no longer a luxury—it’s a mechanical necessity for congestion reduction and safety.

I am particularly interested in the AI in Audit & Inspection Management session on Day 1. Understanding how SAP plans to integrate these tools to move from reactive to proactive risk management is critical for the future of connected logistics hubs.

Could you share if there will be a specific focus on mobile-first inspection scenarios for heavy industrial environments, such as port infrastructure or large-scale warehouse facilities? Looking forward to the potential for these conversations in Potsdam!

entry.title: Re: 16 schedule Line should be maintain in VOV5
entry.content:
Hi @hiteshsinghal26,

The limitation of 10 entries in VOV5 is a hardcoded UI constraint in the standard SAP table TVEPZ.

From an Industrial Systems perspective, having 16 schedule line categories for a single Item Category/MRP Type combination suggests a very high level of process complexity.

I would suggest reviewing the following:

  • Item Category Splitting: Can these 16 scenarios be divided into different Item Categories? This is the standard way to manage complex determination logic.

  • MRP Type Logic: Are all 16 categories relevant for the same MRP type? Differentiating by MRP type usually resolves these 'space' issues in VOV5.

  • Manual Overwrite: If some of these are rare edge cases, they can be manually selected in the sales order, provided they are correctly defined in VOV6.

Expanding this table beyond 10 entries would require a core modification, which goes against the Clean Core strategy. It might be worth re-evaluating the business requirement to see if the process can be optimized.

entry.title: Re: How to Exclude Purchase Account (EIN) from Material L...
entry.content:
Good day @seonjoong 

Thank you for your question.

In your scenario, the posting you are seeing happens when Purchase Account Management and Material Ledger are active. See KBA 2333897 for more insights.

So, the answer is, there is not standard configuration option to exclude the Purchase Account transaction key EIN from Material Ledger closing while keeping Purchase Account Management active for the same material and company code.

The reason is that the Purchase Account is not only a “PO amount only” account from a technical posting perspective. It is also used to document the value at which externally procured materials are posted. If Actual Costing is active, the period end closing calculates and posts variances for materials, which can update the periodic unit price and create settlement postings.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/60a09f68f2444ceca31dcac2e7017945/f8f82d2c3b5142f49a388f1cccd0a2c2.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/7f47a6d9441c46ac86c96fd27f6015f0/7983787263a7480483f3ae8b88d438da.html?locale=en-US

This basically means:

  • If Purchase Account Management is active, the system can post to EIN/EKG for purchase related material postings.
  • If Material Ledger/Actual Costing is active, the system can also post settlement results during ML closing.
  • When both are active, ML post closing can generate postings to the Purchase Account.
  • This is not an error in account determination.
  • It is not possible to configure the Purchase Account to receive only the original GR or invoice purchase amount while excluding ML settlement postings from the same transaction key.
  • Changing this standard posting logic by enhancement or substitution would mean you are not adhering to the clean-core option of the Public Cloud.

See KBA 2518843 which also relates to this situation, explains further on derivation of the value of goods.

For the Material Ledger posting logic itself, note 908737 and KBA 2558888 provides information on Post Closing step in CKMLCP and New Actual Costing.

What I would recommend is:

  • Option 1, instead of trying to prevent ML closing posting, keep the Purchase Account Management active and separate the value in reporting. You can use document source, journal entry type, business transaction type, source document, etc.
  • Option 2, review whether Actual Costing is required for the material or valuation are. Don’t just do this if you only want to avoid posting to the Purchase Account.
  • Alternatively, as option 3, if the account is expected to contain only GR or invoice purchase amounts, then the requirement should be treated as a reporting requirement, not as a posting logic change. The standard posting logic will not support excluding ML settlement for EIN while keeping purchase accounting and actual costing active.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Request to Change Recipient for SuccessFactors Instan...
entry.content:
Hi Team,

To update the recipient for technical instance notifications, you actually don't need to open an incident; this is managed via self-service in SAP for Me.

Instance-related alerts are controlled through the Cloud System Notification Subscriptions (CSNS) tool. You can find this under the Systems & Provisioning tab. Once there, you can search for your specific SuccessFactors Tenant ID and update the email addresses for each notification category (Maintenance, Incidents, etc.).

A few tips to ensure success:

  • S-User Permissions: Ensure the person making the change has the 'Display/Edit Cloud System Notifications' permission.

  • IT Contact Role: Also check the Contact Persons section in SAP for Me. If the old email is listed as the primary 'IT Contact', update that role as well to ensure critical system comms are rerouted.

  • Optimization: As a best practice in Industrial Systems, I recommend using a team distribution list instead of an individual’s email address. This prevents future gaps if there is a change in your internal team.

Hope this helps you get your notifications routed to the right place!

entry.title: Integrating Google Gemini AI with SAPUI5 Frontend using Node.js Backend
entry.content:
Introduction 

Artificial Intelligence is transforming enterprise applications by enabling intelligent user interactions and automation. In this blog, I will demonstrate how to build an AI-powered chat assistant using: 

  • SAPUI5 for frontend UI  
  • Node.js + Express.js for backend service  
  • Google Gemini API for AI-generated responses  

This application allows users to enter queries in a SAPUI5 chat interface, send them to a backend Node.js service, and receive AI-generated responses dynamically. 

 

Architecture / Flow Explanation 

The application follows a 3-layer architecture 

Frontend (SAPUI5) 

  • Capturing user input 
  • Displaying chat messages 
  • Calling backend REST API 

Backend (Node.js / Express) 

  • Receiving frontend request 
  • Processing user prompt 
  • Calling Gemini API 
  • Sending AI response back 

AI Layer (Google Gemini) 

  • Understanding prompt 
  • Generating intelligent response 

 

Frontend Explanation 

View: 

  • ScrollContainer : For scrolling chat history 
  • VBox :To store chat messages dynamically 
  • Input Field:- User question input 
  • Button: Send message
  • View 
<mvc:View controllerName="frontend.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m">

    <Page id="page" title="GNI Assistant" enableScrolling="false">

        <content>
            <VBox class="chatContainer" height="100%" justifyContent="SpaceBetween">

            <ScrollContainer height="100%" vertical="true">
                <VBox id="chatBox" class="chatMessages" width="100%" />
            </ScrollContainer>
            

        </VBox>
        <HBox class="chatInputBar">
           
                <Input id="inputField"
                    placeholder="Ask anything..."
                    width="800px"
                    submit="onAskAI"/>

                <Button icon="sap-icon://paper-plane"
                    type="Emphasized"
                    press="onAskAI" />
            </HBox>
        </content>

    </Page>
</mvc:View>

 

Controller Flow Explanation 

SAPUI5 Controller Logic: 

const sInput = this.byId("inputField").getValue(); -> Retrieves text entered by user. 

  • Displays user message aligned to right. 
           const oUserMsg = new sap.m.HBox({
                justifyContent: "End",
                items: [
                    new sap.m.Text({ text: sInput }).addStyleClass("userMessage")
                ]
            });

 

Shows temporary loading message while backend processes request 

  oChatBox.addItem(oUserMsg);
            this.byId("inputField").setValue("");
            const oThinkingBox = new sap.m.Text({
                text: "Thinking..."
            }).addStyleClass("aiMessage");

            oChatBox.addItem(oThinkingBox);

Sends POST request to backend 

const response = await fetch("http://localhost:3000/mcp/query", {
                    method: "POST",
                    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
                    body: JSON.stringify({ input: sInput })
                });
                const data = await response.json();

Controller.js:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
], (Controller) => {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("frontned.controller.View1", {
        /**
         * @override
         * @returns {void|undefined}
         */
        onInit: function () {

        },
        onAskAI: async function () {
            const sInput = this.byId("inputField").getValue();
            const oChatBox = this.byId("chatBox");

            if (!sInput) return;
            const oUserMsg = new sap.m.HBox({
                justifyContent: "End",
                items: [
                    new sap.m.Text({ text: sInput }).addStyleClass("userMessage")
                ]
            });

            oChatBox.addItem(oUserMsg);
            this.byId("inputField").setValue("");
            const oThinkingBox = new sap.m.Text({
                text: "Thinking..."
            }).addStyleClass("aiMessage");

            oChatBox.addItem(oThinkingBox);

            try {
                const response = await fetch("http://localhost:3000/mcp/query", {
                    method: "POST",
                    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
                    body: JSON.stringify({ input: sInput })
                });
                const data = await response.json();
                console.log("Full API Data:", data);
                let sRawAiMessage = data.message || data.response || data.data || JSON.stringify(data);

                sRawAiMessage = sRawAiMessage.replace(/</g, "<").replace(/>/g, ">");

                let cleanMessage = sRawAiMessage.replace(/```javascript/g, "").replace(/```/g, "").trim();

                oChatBox.removeItem(oThinkingBox);

                var textV = new sap.m.Text()
                textV.setText(cleanMessage)
                textV.addStyleClass("aiMessage")

                const oAiMsg = new sap.m.HBox({
                    justifyContent: "Start",
                    items: [
                        textV
                    ]
                });

                oChatBox.addItem(oAiMsg);

                setTimeout(() => {
                    const oScroll = this.byId("chatBox").getParent().getDomRef();
                    if (oScroll) {
                        oScroll.scrollTop = oScroll.scrollHeight;
                    }
                }, 100);

            } catch (error) {
                console.error("ERROR:", error);

                oChatBox.removeItem(oThinkingBox);

                oChatBox.addItem(new sap.m.Text({
                    text: " Backend connection failed"
                }).addStyleClass("aiMessage"));
            }
        }
    });
});

Note: The backend URL currently points to localhost for local development.
For production or cloud deployment, update this endpoint with your deployed backend URL

 

Backend Explanation 

AiService.js: 

The aiService.js file is responsible for handling communication between the backend server and the Google Gemini AI API. 
It receives user input, sends it to Gemini, and returns the generated response. 

Step 1: 
Import Gemini Library 

const { GoogleGenerativeAI } = require("@google/generative-ai");

Before using any external package in Node.js, we must import it into our file. 

Here, we are importing the Google Gemini SDK library provided by Google. 

This library contains built-in methods to: 

  • Connect with Gemini AI service  
  • Authenticate using API key  
  • Select AI models  
  • Generate responses from prompts 

GoogleGenerativeAI: 

It is the main class provided by Gemini SDK. 

We use this class to create a Gemini client instance later in the code 

Step 2: 

require("dotenv").config();

This line loads environment variables from the .env file. 

Environment variables are used to store sensitive data like: 

  • API keys  
  • Database passwords  
  • Secret tokens 

Step 3:  

Create Gemini Instance 

const genAI = new GoogleGenerativeAI(process.env.GEMINI_API_KEY);
  • After importing the Gemini SDK, we create a Gemini client instance to establish secure communication with the Google AI service.
  • This is used to Authenticate with Google and Establish connection with Gemini AI 
  • We pass API key here, this tells Google this application is authorized to use Gemini 

Step 4: 

Select Gemini Model 

This specifies which AI model should process the request

  const model = genAI.getGenerativeModel({
            model: "gemini-2.5-flash-lite"
        });

 

Step 5: 

Build Prompt

  • AI cannot generate response unless we send instructions 
  • So that we are giving instructions to the AI 
const prompt = `
                    You are a highly intelligent, adaptive, and user-friendly AI assistant.

                    Your goal is to understand the user's intent and respond in the best possible way.

                    -----------------------------------
                    CORE BEHAVIOR
                    -----------------------------------
                    - Analyze the user's intent before answering
                    - Adapt format dynamically (like ChatGPT)
                    - Be clear, concise, and useful
                    - Do not force a fixed format

                    -----------------------------------
                    RESPONSE STYLE RULES
                    -----------------------------------

                    1. SIMPLE QUESTIONS (e.g., 2+2, capital of India)
                    - Return only the direct answer
                    - No explanation

                    2. PROGRAMMING / CODE
                    - Return only code
                    - No explanation outside code
                    - Keep it clean and minimal
                    - Add comments only if useful

                    SPECIAL CASE (IMPORTANT):
                    - If the request is about arrays, objects, JSON:
                    → Always return as proper code with variable declaration
                    → Example: const data = [...]

                    3. DATA FORMAT CONVERSION (VERY IMPORTANT)
                    - If user asks to convert (JSON ↔ XML ↔ JS object):
                    → Return ONLY the converted format
                    → No explanation
                    → Keep it clean and valid

                    4. FACTS / NEWS / GENERAL INFORMATION
                    - Use headings and bullet points
                    - Keep it structured and readable

                    5. SCIENCE / THEORY / EDUCATION
                    - Explain clearly in simple terms
                    - Use sections if needed
                    - Include formulas if relevant

                    6. MATHEMATICS
                    - If simple → return direct answer
                    - If complex → show step-by-step solution
                    - Keep it clean

                    7. STORY / CREATIVE WRITING
                    - Use paragraph format
                    - Follow word limit strictly
                    - Make it natural and engaging

                    8. USER-SPECIFIC FORMAT
                    - If user gives format, follow it exactly

                    9. ANY OTHER REQUEST
                    - Choose best format automatically
                    - Prioritize clarity and usefulness

                    -----------------------------------
                    STRICT RULES
                    -----------------------------------
                    - Never return code unless it's a coding request
                    - Never return JSON unless explicitly asked
                    - Never mix explanation with code
                    - Do not over-explain simple queries
                    - Always match user intent

                    -----------------------------------

                    User Request: ${input}`;

        const result = await model.generateContent(prompt);
        const response = await result.response;

        return response.text();

Step 6: 

Create .env file: 

Here we need to specify API Key 

GEMINI_API_KEY=AIzaSyBB**********************

To Create API Key: 

  • Click on this link: https://aistudio.google.com/u/1/api-keys?project=gen-lang-client-0621332950 
  • Click on Porjects on left side bar and then click on Create a New Project and provide project name and create a project 

 

  • Then click on API keys and then click on Create API key and select project and create API key  
  • Then copy API key and paste it in .env file 

 

 service.js:

require("dotenv").config();
const express = require("express");
const cors = require("cors");
const mcpRoutes = require("./routes/mcpRoutes");

const app = express();
app.use(cors());
app.use(express.json());

app.use("/mcp", mcpRoutes);

const PORT = 3000;
app.listen(PORT, () => {
    console.log(`GAI Server running on http://localhost:${PORT}`);
});

 

routes/mcpRoutes.js:

const express = require("express");
const router = express.Router();
const { getAIResponse } = require("../services/aiService");
router.post("/query", async (req, res) => {
    try {
        const { input } = req.body;
        const aiReply = await getAIResponse(input);

        res.json({
            success: true,
            message: aiReply
        });
    } catch (err) {
        res.status(500).json({ error: err.message });
    }
});

module.exports = router;

 

service/aiService.js:

const { GoogleGenerativeAI } = require("@google/generative-ai");

require("dotenv").config();

const genAI = new GoogleGenerativeAI(process.env.GEMINI_API_KEY);

async function getAIResponse(input) {
    try {
        const model = genAI.getGenerativeModel({
            model: "gemini-2.5-flash-lite"
        });
        const prompt = `
                    You are a highly intelligent, adaptive, and user-friendly AI assistant.

                    Your goal is to understand the user's intent and respond in the best possible way.

                    -----------------------------------
                    CORE BEHAVIOR
                    -----------------------------------
                    - Analyze the user's intent before answering
                    - Adapt format dynamically (like ChatGPT)
                    - Be clear, concise, and useful
                    - Do not force a fixed format

                    -----------------------------------
                    RESPONSE STYLE RULES
                    -----------------------------------

                    1. SIMPLE QUESTIONS (e.g., 2+2, capital of India)
                    - Return only the direct answer
                    - No explanation

                    2. PROGRAMMING / CODE
                    - Return only code
                    - No explanation outside code
                    - Keep it clean and minimal
                    - Add comments only if useful

                    SPECIAL CASE (IMPORTANT):
                    - If the request is about arrays, objects, JSON:
                    → Always return as proper code with variable declaration
                    → Example: const data = [...]

                    3. DATA FORMAT CONVERSION (VERY IMPORTANT)
                    - If user asks to convert (JSON ↔ XML ↔ JS object):
                    → Return ONLY the converted format
                    → No explanation
                    → Keep it clean and valid

                    4. FACTS / NEWS / GENERAL INFORMATION
                    - Use headings and bullet points
                    - Keep it structured and readable

                    5. SCIENCE / THEORY / EDUCATION
                    - Explain clearly in simple terms
                    - Use sections if needed
                    - Include formulas if relevant

                    6. MATHEMATICS
                    - If simple → return direct answer
                    - If complex → show step-by-step solution
                    - Keep it clean

                    7. STORY / CREATIVE WRITING
                    - Use paragraph format
                    - Follow word limit strictly
                    - Make it natural and engaging

                    8. USER-SPECIFIC FORMAT
                    - If user gives format, follow it exactly

                    9. ANY OTHER REQUEST
                    - Choose best format automatically
                    - Prioritize clarity and usefulness

                    -----------------------------------
                    STRICT RULES
                    -----------------------------------
                    - Never return code unless it's a coding request
                    - Never return JSON unless explicitly asked
                    - Never mix explanation with code
                    - Do not over-explain simple queries
                    - Always match user intent

                    -----------------------------------

                    User Request: ${input}`;

        const result = await model.generateContent(prompt);
        const response = await result.response;

        return response.text();

    } catch (err) {
        console.error("GEMINI ERROR:", err.message);
        return "I hit a snag. Please try again!";
    }
}

module.exports = { getAIResponse };


Output:

 

 

Architecture

FRONTEND (UI5 App)
frontend/
│
├── webapp/
│   ├── controller/
│   │   ├── App.controller.js
│   │   └── View1.controller.js
│   │
│   ├── view/
│   │   ├── App.view.xml
│   │   └── View1.view.xml
│   │
│   ├── model/
│   ├── css/
│   ├── i18n/
│   │
│   ├── Component.js
│   ├── manifest.json
│   └── index.html
│
├── ui5.yaml
├── package.json
BACKEND
backend/
│
├── db/
│   └── schema.cds
│
├── srv/
│   ├── service.cds
│   ├── handlers.js
│   └── server.js
│
├── package.json

Css

.userMessage {
    background: #007aff;
    color: white;
    padding: 8px 12px ;
    border-radius: 10px;
    margin-top: 120px;
    max-width: 1000%;
}
.chatContainer {
    height: 100%;
}


.sapMScrollCont {
    flex: 1;
}

.chatInputBar {
    flex-shrink: 0;
    padding: 30px;
    border-top: 1px solid #ccc;
    background: white;
}

.aiMessage {
    background: #e8e8e7e2;
    padding: 12px;
    border-radius: 10px;
    margin: 5px;
    max-width: 85%; 
    display: inline-block;
    word-break: break-word;
    color: rgba(10, 9, 9, 0.815);
}

.aiMessage pre {
    background-color: #2d2d2d;
    color: #f8f8f2;
    padding: 10px;
    border-radius: 5px;
    overflow-x: auto;
    font-family: "Courier New", Courier, monospace;
    margin: 10px 0;
}

.aiMessage code {
    font-family: "Courier New", Courier, monospace;
    background-color: rgba(0,0,0,0.05);
    padding: 2px 4px;
    border-radius: 3px;
}
.btnClass{
    margin-left: 700px;
}

Conclusion

This project demonstrates how SAPUI5 applications can be enhanced with modern AI capabilities by integrating Google Gemini through a Node.js backend service.

By separating the frontend, backend, and AI layers, the application remains scalable, maintainable, and easy to extend for future enterprise use cases.

During development, one key learning was the importance of prompt engineering in controlling AI response formatting and improving user experience.

Possible future enhancements for this application include:

• Maintaining conversation history for contextual chat
• Streaming AI responses in real time
• Deploying backend services to SAP BTP or cloud platforms
• Adding authentication and user-specific personalization

Integrating AI into enterprise applications opens exciting possibilities for intelligent automation, smart assistants, and enhanced productivity within SAP ecosystems.

entry.title: Re: We are unable to find solution for the scope item 1BS...
entry.content:
Hi @karim45,

The missing PDF in the Accelerators tab for the 2025-FPS0 version is likely due to the documentation still being finalized for that specific release.

Resolution Steps:

  • Switch Versions: Toggle the version filter back to 2023. The core configuration for 1BS (Accounting for Incoming Sales Orders) is highly stable, and the 2023 PDF will provide the setup steps you need.

  • Download from 'All Versions': If you click on the scope item title, check if there is an 'All Versions' link; sometimes the document is only attached to the 'Latest' general release rather than the FPS-specific sub-version.

In my experience with Industrial Systems and S/4HANA Cloud, using the documentation from one release prior is a safe 'Clean Core' workaround while waiting for the latest FPS indexing to complete.

Hope this gets your project moving again!

entry.title: Re: Managing Parent-Child Hierarchies in SAC: A Datasphere-Driven Approach
entry.content:
Thanks for your response @RACHID_AITBOUALE ! The empty nodes are a real issue because of the disaggregation feature. The property filter works very well in our case but it's something you always need to remember when building Stories.

I see a real chance for change with seamless planning. Let's hope SAP takes it!
