entry.title: EWM Inbound process with POSC - Deconsolidation
entry.content:
EWM Inbound process with POSC - Deconsolidation

Introduction:

This is the most common scenario where, vendor sends a single pallet containing five different SKUs. And we can't put the whole pallet in one bin because SKU A goes to Cold Storage, SKU B goes to General Shelving, and SKU C is Hazardous for example. This POSC solution directs the pallet to a Deconsolidation Work Center. There, a worker scans the items and "re-packs" them into smaller, destination-specific HUs and moved to respective storage bins.

And this deconsolidation steps are applicable for certain products from specific vendors where vendors usually sends the different SKU's within single pallet. This will be control by process type determination indicator.  For standard products direct putawy can be carried out.


Configurations Steps:

  1. Define Warehouse Process type
  2. Determine Process type determination indicator
  3. Determine Warehouse process type
  4. Putaway Strategies

4.1 Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

4.2 Assign Storage Types to Storage Type Search Sequence

4.3 Define Putaway Control Indicator

4.4 Specify Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

4.5 Optimize Access Strategy for Storage Type Search: Putaway

  1. Define Process Oriented Storage Control

5.1 Define external steps

5.2 Storage process - Definition

5.3 Assign Storage step process to Storage process

5.4 Process Oriented Storage Control - Setup

  1. Specify Work center Layout
  2. Define Work Center
  3. Assign bin to Work Center
  4. Assign Storage bin to Activity areas
  5. Deconsolidation in Goods receipt process

10.1 Define Attributes of deconsolidation

10.2 Specify deconsolidation station

10.3 Assign number range intervals to consolidation groups

 

Configuration Path:

  1. Define Warehouse Process type

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define warehouse process type

Standard Warehouse Porcess type for putawy-PW02

The movement the Goods are arrived from vendor it will be approached in the door and from the unloading will be done to move the pallet for deconsolidation.

Internal warehouse process types used in POSC 3060 and 3065

  1. Determine Process type determination indicator

 Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define Control Indicators for Determining Warehouse Process Types

  1. Determine Warehouse process type

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Determine Warehouse Process Type

  1. Putaway Strategies

4.1 Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process ->Strategies -> Storage Type Search-> Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

4.2 Assign Storage Types to Storage Type Search Sequence

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process ->Strategies -> Storage Type Search-> Assign Storage Types to Storage Type Search Sequence

4.3 Define Putaway Control Indicator

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process ->Strategies -> Storage Type Search-> Define Putaway Control Indicator

4.4 Specify Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process-> Strategies -> Storage Type Search-> Specify Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

4.5 Optimize Access Strategy for Storage Type Search: Putaway

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process ->Strategies -> Storage Type Search-> Optimize Access Strategy for Storage Type Search: Putaway

  1. Define Process Oriented Storage Control

5.1 Define external steps

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define Process-Oriented Storage Control

5.2 Storage process - Definition

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define Process-Oriented Storage Control

5.3 Assign Storage step process to Storage process

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define Process-Oriented Storage Control

Make sure to assign this Storage process to the Warehouse process type PW02

5.4 Process Oriented Storage Control - setup

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define Process-Oriented Storage Control

  1. Specify Work center Layout

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM ->Master data -> Work Center -> Specify Work Center Layout

  1. Define Work Center

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM ->Master data -> Work Center -> Define Work Center

  1. Assign bin to Work Center

Tcode: /N/SCWM/TWORKST

  1. Assign Storage bin to Activity areas

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM ->Master data -> Activity Areas -> Assign Storage Bins to Activity Areas.

  1. Deconsolidation in Goods receipt process

10.1 Define Attributes of deconsolidation

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process -> Deconsolidation-> Define Attributes for Deconsolidation

10.2 Specify Deconsolidation Station

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process -> Deconsolidation-> Specify Deconsolidation Station

10.3 Assign number range intervals to Consolidation Groups

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process -> Deconsolidation-> Assign Number Range Intervals to Consolidation Groups

Testing:

Test Materials are 17H1-EWM-03, 17H1-EWM-04, 17H1-EWM-05

Make sure we have assigned Process type determination indicator PS has been assigned

Inbound delivery has been created and distributed to EWM

IBD: 180106588

Access Inbound delivery in EWM /n/SCWM/PRDI

All 3 Products are packed in single pallet.

Create warehouse task for unloading from Door

Now lets access this Inbound delivery in Monitor /n/SCWM/MON

As you can see 4 different tasks are created, where one task 221 is open and other tasks are in status B - Waiting

Lets confirm the open task,

The moment the task is confirmed, new task has been created for Deconsolidation

Lets confirm the Deconsolidation task, and perform the deconsolidation activity

Access Transaction code /n/SCWM/DCONS

input details as below and execute

We can a pallet with 3 Products

Now create 3 individual HU to pack individually.

991000000037; 991000000038; 991000000039

Select task and click ok and click on execute button to pack into destination HU. Repeat the steps for other 2 Products

3 Products are packed to individual HU in Deconsolidation work center.

Now, Go back to monitor and refresh the delivery.

Now final task from Deconsolidation bin to final bin is open to confirm

After confirmation of the tasks, putaway will be completed and stock is updated in the designated bins successfully.

As we mentioned earlier, this deconsolidation process has been controlled for specific products from specific vendor with process type determination indicator which is maintained in the Product Master.

For receiving of standard products are straight forward process,  example for product 17H1-EWM-01, there is no Process type determination indicator maintained.

Putaway completed and stock is updated in designated Storage bin.

 

Conclusion:

This client requirement is effectively addressed through the implementation of SAP EWM Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC) with Deconsolidation, which provides the necessary flexibility and control for handling complex inbound shipments. By leveraging POSC, the warehouse can systematically manage mixed‑SKU vendor pallets that cannot be safely or accurately stored through standard direct putaway.

The deconsolidation step converts bulk inbound Handling Units into destination‑specific HUs, ensuring that materials with special storage requirements—such as cold chain items, hazardous materials, or regulated products—are automatically routed to their respective storage types or work centers. This structured transition significantly improves bin-level inventory accuracy and eliminates the risk of incorrect or unsafe storage.

The use of the Process Type Determination Indicator plays a key role in differentiating complex mixed pallets from simple single‑SKU loads. As a result, POSC activities are triggered only when required, ensuring that standard inbound flows continue uninterrupted while operational resources are efficiently allocated to deconsolidation scenarios. The POSC Setup, minimizes manual decision‑making on the warehouse floor, reduces handling errors, and ensures consistent execution of putaway rules.

 

***End of the Document***

entry.title: Re: Integrating Google Gemini AI with SAPUI5 Frontend using Node.js Backend
entry.content:
Great work, @Hari_Kumar_Talari! The way you've structured the prompt instructions to handle different data formats (JSON vs. XML) is a massive win for developer productivity.

From my perspective in Industrial Systems Engineering, seeing this kind of AI integration move toward a Clean Core strategy is exactly what the ecosystem needs. By offloading the AI logic to a Node.js middleware, you keep the SAPUI5 frontend lightweight and maintainable.

Quick question: Have you considered using SAP BTP Destinations to secure that Gemini API key for a production-grade deployment? Looking forward to your next post!

entry.title: Re: CPI Package Design – How do you structure packages for cross-domain integrations?
entry.content:
Hi @MichaelJohn88,

In my experience with Industrial Systems and high-scale logistics corridors, the most robust approach is Business Domain supplemented by Capability packages.

For your specific requirement, I’d recommend creating a new 'Document Management & Analytics' package. Here’s why:

  • Scalability: Today it’s procurement contracts, but tomorrow your AI platform might need HR or Legal documents. Keeping it in a 'Procurement' package will eventually lead to confusing cross-domain dependencies.

  • Security: Azure Blob and OpenText connectivity often involve different security credentials and certificates than your S/4HANA-Ariba pipe. A separate package allows you to apply tighter Access Policies specifically for the document layer.

Split Criteria: I generally recommend splitting when a package hits the Access Policy boundary. If the people managing 'Vendor Master' shouldn't have visibility into 'AI Contract Analytics,' they shouldn't be in the same package.

Keeping your 'Procurement' package for domain-specific transactions (POs, Invoices) while using 'Capability' packages for cross-cutting services (Document flows) is the best way to maintain a Clean Core.

entry.title: Media Handling from UI5 Application using OData V2
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

Hope you are doing well.

In this blog, I will walk you through how to upload images to a database table, retrieve them, and delete them using an OData V2 service in an SAP UI5 application.

While working on a real-time scenario (like managing employee data), I initially tried storing both employee details and images in a single table. However, this approach was not effective. Instead, I implemented a better design by using two separate tables:

  1. One for storing employee details
  2. Another for storing image files

The Employee ID acts as the primary key in the employee table, and in the image table, the filename (mapped to Employee ID) is used as the primary key. This approach ensures better data organization and maintainability.

For this implementation, I used an OData V2 service:
ZGSHBLOGMEDIAPROJECT_SRV, along with a SAP UI5 Master-Detail application.

The application follows a Master-Detail pattern:

  • Master Page: Displays all images available in the database
  • Detail Page: Shows the selected image along with actions like upload and delete

 

When a user selects an image from the master page, the corresponding data is fetched and displayed on the detail page.

READ (GET) Operation

When a user clicks on an image in the master page, the selected item’s path is retrieved and bound to the UI using element binding.

onSelectItemsInMasterPage: function (oEvent) {
    var oSelectedItem = oEvent.getSource().getSelectedContextPaths()[0];
   this.byId("mastePageSimpleForm").bindElement(`mediaModel>${oSelectedItem}`);
}

To display the image, a formatter function constructs the correct OData URL:

fetchImage: function (filename) {
    return `/sap/opu/odata/SAP/ZGSHBLOGMEDIAPROJECT_SRV/blogmediaSet('${filename}')/$value`;
}

This URL is bound to the src property of the Image control, allowing the image to be fetched and displayed.

CREATE Operation (Upload Image)

In the detail page, I provided:

  • An input field for image name
  • A file uploader control

When the user selects a file and clicks upload, the following function is triggered:

onUploadImage: function () {
    var oModel = this.getView().getModel("mediaModel");
    var oFileUploader = this.byId("idFileUploader");
    var sFileName = this.byId("nameOfTheImage").getValue();

    if (!oFileUploader.getValue()) {
        MessageToast.show("Please choose a file");
        return;
    }

    oFileUploader.removeAllHeaderParameters();

    oFileUploader.addHeaderParameter(new sap.ui.unified.FileUploaderParameter({
        name: "SLUG",
        value: sFileName
    }));

    oFileUploader.addHeaderParameter(new sap.ui.unified.FileUploaderParameter({
        name: "x-csrf-token",
        value: oModel.getSecurityToken()
    }));

    oFileUploader.addHeaderParameter(new sap.ui.unified.FileUploaderParameter({
        name: "Content-Type",
        value: oFileUploader.type
    }));

    oFileUploader.addHeaderParameter(new sap.ui.unified.FileUploaderParameter({
        name: 'X-Requested-With',
        value: 'XMLHttpRequest',
    }));

    oFileUploader.upload();

    MessageToast.show("Image Uploaded Successfully!");
}

This handles the upload using proper headers like CSRF token, Content-Type, and SLUG (filename).

DELETE Operation

A delete button is provided in the detail page toolbar.

When clicked:

  1. A fragment opens showing all images
  2. The user selects an image
  3. Clicks delete to remove it from the database
onPressDeleteImageButtoninDeleteFargment: function () {
    var sPath = sap.ui.getCore().byId('listInDeleteImageFragment').getSelectedContextPaths()[0];
    var sPath2 = `${sPath}/$value`;

    this.getView().getModel("mediaModel").remove(sPath2, {
        method: 'Delete',
        success: function () {
            MessageToast.show("Image Deleted Successfully!");
        },
        error: function () {
            MessageToast.show("Error while deleting the image");
        }
    });
}

This uses the selected item's primary key to delete the image from the backend.

 

In this blog, we explored how to implement image handling in an SAP UI5 application using OData V2. By separating employee data and image data into different tables and linking them through a key (Employee ID), we achieve a cleaner and more scalable design.

We covered the complete flow of uploading, retrieving, and deleting images, which closely aligns with standard CRUD operations, with slight differences in handling media content.

Once you are comfortable with OData CRUD operations, implementing media handling becomes straightforward. This approach can be easily extended to real-world applications where managing files alongside business data is required.

Thank you for reading!

entry.title: AI-Driven Exploratory Testing (AET) – A Tester’s Point of View
entry.content:
AI-Driven Exploratory Testing (AET) – A Tester’s Point of View

By Akansha Bhadani
Team: Procurement Test Team, BS-F&S-CDX-ECF S/4HANA Test Engineering.

 

Introduction: Enhancing Test Efficiency with AI

The AI-Driven Exploratory Testing (AET) tool is an in-house innovation of the S/4HANA Test Engineering Team, developed to transform the way exploratory testing is performed. It leverages various Exploratory Test Tours along with application insights from the SAP What’s New page to automatically generate structured test instructions by seamlessly combining functionality with exploratory approaches.

In today’s fast-evolving testing landscape, improving efficiency and ensuring comprehensive coverage remain constant challenges. AI-Driven Exploratory Testing (AET) emerges as a solution aimed at addressing these gaps by bringing structure and intelligence into exploratory testing practices.

Before adopting AET, testing largely relied on manual, ad-hoc approaches without a defined methodology—leading to inconsistencies and inefficiencies.

Challenges with Traditional Testing Approaches

Manual testing relied on ad-hoc, unstructured approaches without a defined methodology. This created several problems:

  • Onboarding Challenges: New team members struggled to identify starting points and navigate the various exploratory testing tours, resulting in incomplete test coverage and missed critical workflows.
  • Knowledge gaps: Experienced testers who had plateaued in their engagement or were juggling multiple assignments often lost familiarity with the product. This required them to reacquaint themselves with testing tours, making it time-consuming to recall procedures and validate quality—ultimately delaying their ability to meet testing targets.

How AET Transforms Exploratory Testing

  • New testers can easily identify testing entry points across exploratory testing tours, it provides testers with a structured checklist that serves as a guide, ensuring all key features are systematically covered during testing.
  • The Novelty Score feature in Delta Test Flows is a good feature in understanding the gap in our automate. Testers can enhance their existing automate or test the functionalities manually which will ensure the product quality.

        Key Advantages:

  • Clear testing entry points
  • Systematic feature coverage
  • Standardized guidelines for all testers
  • Reduced dependency on tester expertise

 AET vs Traditional Testing: A Shift in Approach

While it's true that some UI and functional issues can be discovered through direct or exploratory testing but, AET provides a more structured, systematic, and guided approach to test execution and defect identification.

Aspect

Without AET

With AET

Discovery

Manual testing can still find issues

Few issues can be common but, it provides more comprehensive coverage.

Approach

Ad-hoc, intuition-based

Structured, repeatable, methodology driven.

Consistency

Variable across testers

Standardized and consistent.

Key Takeaway:

AI Tools such as AET are not mandatory for finding defects. These Tools enhance the testing methodology by providing:

  • Systematic coverage tracking
  • Regression test automation
  • Consistent documentation
  • Faster feedback loops

AI Tools complement human testing—they don't replace the need for tester expertise.

Hands-on Experience with AET in IET Cycles

IET4* – Some examples of issues that AET helped me unearth

Example1:  To Test the Delete feature of MPS

Incident : DINC0841115 

Priority : Medium

 

Brief Description about the Issue

In the App Manage Model Product Specification, Select the MPS which is already Deleted and attempt to create a document (contract here). At first instance the option for document creation is disabled but if the user again navigates back from Object page to List Report the option to create document get enabled.

How AET is helpful

You need to create MPS , mark it to delete and follow the approach which is suggested, or you can test with the existing values available. Here with the help of AET I got the exact use case to be tested.

Issue Encountered:

 

Example 2: To test the Item Details for the App Confirm Receipt of Goods

Incident : DINC0845639 

Priority: Medium

 

Brief Description of the issue:

In the App Confirm Receipt of Goods, I am checking the details of the Items along with the Supplier details. upon hovering the cursor on supplier field the hyperlink is not functional.

How AET is helpful

As per the recommended flow in AET , I need to check the Supplier name link, realized that the supplier detail is not in the hyperlink format. There are n number of links in an app which becomes challenging to validate sometimes.

Issue Encountered:

 

 

Example 3:  To Test the Status field for the app Manage Supplier Quotation

Incident : DINC0846438 

Priority- Low

 

Brief Description of the issue:

For the App Manage Supplier Quotation, I tested around the Status field where I encountered the missing validation for the field.

How AET is helpful:

There are multiple fields in each app, for example given in screenshot the two fields beside works well with validation in place but upon following the flows generated from the tool I end up testing the status field and encountered the related issue.

 

Issue Encountered:

 

 

 

Example 4: To test the Personalization for Limit Items PR

Incident : DINC0845648 

Priority- Medium

 

Brief Description of the issue:

I uploaded the screenshot of the Create PR app and prompted to generate few test flows where I encountered an issue related to Limit Items settings.

How AET is helpful

Tried testing the upload feature in AET studio, created limit item PR and then encountered the issue because the already existing PR had only Items facet.

 

Error Encountered :

 

 

 

IET3*- Some examples of issues that AET helped me unearth

Example 1 :  To test the Sort options for App Sources of Supply for field Sources.

Incident : DINC0799841

Priority :  High

 

Brief Description of the issue:

For the App Manage Sources of Supply as proposed by AET test flows , tried sorting the Supplier (Source) here and encountered the application crashed.

How AET is helpful:

The App Manage Sources of supply is quite stable and no major issues occur. As a tester, I explored sort option based on AET generated flows and could get valid issue which led to me to encounter an exception.

 

Issue Encountered:

 

 

 

 

Example 2 : To Test the generated List in App Manage Purchase Order

Incident: DINC0799711

Priority: Very High

 

Brief Description of the issue:

The App Manage Purchase order where the PO is created and navigated back to List report , upon clicking on Go the list looked empty which was a clear issue

 

How AET is helpful:

The Automate for Manage Purchase Order include creation of PO , navigation back and forth is not included ,by following the steps proposed by flows could figure out the issue.

 

Issue Encountered:

 

 

Example 3: To Test the Value help for Purchasing group in App Redistribute Workload

Incident : DINC0801782

Priority : Medium

 

Brief Description of the issue:

 

For the App Redistribute Workload I followed the proposed flows which suggested user to test the value help for Purchasing group field, upon testing it further encountered the issue with the improper filter count.

How AET is helpful:

The expected result mentioned in the AET flows asks the user to validate the column detail where I noticed that the details are getting populated but with incorrect item count. There are chances for Apps having multiple fields and F4 option, end user might miss testing it in detail. AET gave a direction here

 

Issue Encountered:


 

 

 

 

 

Improving Stakeholder Engagement

AET also contributes to better alignment with stakeholders:

  • Helps prioritize critical issues over low-priority ones such as minor UI inconsistencies
  • Enables testers to focus on issues aligned with stakeholder expectations
  • Reduces unnecessary noise in defect reporting

AET Studio: Enabling Smarter Validation

AET Studio plays a crucial role in reducing ambiguity during testing by allowing independent validation.

Let me explain you with few examples how this is possible:

Example 1 : trigger the Empty result behave for an app and check the generated result.

 

Brief Description (Supplier Evaluation Score Output):

The Flow recommend user to test the app with values which will yield empty result, so upon entering the currency which can trigger empty result I found SQL error.

Now, the Tester’s confusion is whether it is a valid issue or not.

I used AET Studio where I entered prompt which can yield me same SQL error ,it generated a desired flow which is an expected behaviour . To some extent now there is a clarity that Analytical apps these kind of issue is expected as there is a mismatch in currency conversion and hence the error.

  •  

  •  

 

 

 

Example 2: To Test the Revoke Functionality for app Manage SES.

Incident : DINC0846402 

Priority : Medium

 

Brief Description (Manage Service Entry Sheet-Lean Services):

Same approach is taken for the App SES whee I tried testing for one of the flow generated from AET .The Revoke Approval button found out to be in active state and also upon clicking it , the application was not responding. I cross checked with AET Studio about the active state of Revoke button and requested the dev team to validate the same(ideally without entering the values the button should not remain enabled).

One side of AET studio is it could be helpful in validating Tester’s confusion and giving a clarity.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Example 3: System is unable to do automatic assignment of processor through value help.

Incident : To be created

Priority: Medium

 

Brief Description (Process PR centrally).

The Assignment of the Processor through value help should happen automatically when the user takes the control over there, here the cursor is already seen due to which user can easily modify values which is not expected.

How AET Studio is helpful :

Prompts can be provided to generate flows and test the application further as per user requirement.

 

*(IET: Stands for Intelligent Exploratory Tests)

Conclusion :

AET continues to evolve and demonstrate strong potential. What initially seemed uncertain became a valuable asset through consistent usage across multiple IET cycles.

Today, it has become an integral part of the testing workflow—used alongside daily activities to guide, validate, and enhance testing efforts.

For testers adapting to AI-driven methodologies, AET represents a meaningful advancement and a reliable support system in navigating complex testing landscapes.

 

 

                        *Disclaimer: The observations and benefits quoted in this blog are entirely the experiential hands-on learnings by me. *

entry.title: SAP Build Deployment Timeout (Again)

The ongoing nature of these disruptions is having a direct impact on our operations, as we continue to lose critical business time and miss important release windows.

Given the recurring pattern, we would appreciate guidance on the recommended course of action from SAP’s side. In particular, we are looking for:

Clarification on the root cause of these periodic timeouts
Any preventive measures or best practices we should adopt
A concrete plan to mitigate or permanently resolve this issue

Prompt attention to this matter would be appreciated, as it is significantly affecting our operational reliability.

entry.title: What is variant configuration and when should it be used?
entry.content:
What is Variant Configuration (and When Should You Use It)?

If you’ve ever tried to order coffee with more than three modifiers—extra hot, half-caf, oat milk, two pumps hazelnut—you already understand product complexity. Now imagine doing that with forklifts, machinery, or electronics and trying to keep your master data from bursting into tears. That’s where SAP Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC) comes in: it’s the difference between a chaotic spreadsheet of doom and a tidy, rules-driven model that says, “Don’t worry, I’ve got this.”

 

The Big Idea (No Bullet Points, Promise)

Advanced Variant Configuration lets you represent countless product possibilities using one configurable material. Instead of creating a separate material for every “diesel + 45 performance + 3‑ton + blue + camera” combo, you define characteristics (like engine type, performance, tonnage, color, and accessories), group them logically, and wire them together with rules that keep things sensible. You also build a Super BOM that contains all potential components and a Super Routing with all potential operations. The system then does the heavy lifting: given a customer’s selection, it picks the right parts and steps automatically. You keep your master data lean, your sales team fast, and your production team off your back.

How It Works (In Real Life)

It starts with modeling. You create a configurable material, define the characteristics your customers care about, and organize them into a class made for configuration. You then build one master bill of materials—the Super BOM—that includes every component any variant might need, and a routing that covers all possible operations. Finally, you add object dependencies—rules that clarify what goes with what, so “electric + 55 performance + 5‑ton” doesn’t sneak past quality like a cat through an open door.

When a sales order comes in, the salesperson selects the values the customer wants. SAP looks at those choices, applies the rules, explodes the Super BOM, and produces a variant-specific structure and routing that production can actually build. No guesswork. No “which one did we mean?” Slack threads. Just the right components, every time.

A Forklift Walks Into a Showroom…

Picture a forklift product line with three drive types (diesel, gas, electric), multiple performance levels, tonnage from one to five tons, several colors, and a menu of accessories. The number of combinations balloons fast—into the tens of thousands if you let it. Without AVC, you’d need a material for each variant, plus a BOM and routing for each. Congratulations, you now manage a small library of nearly identical records that differ on line ten, footnote two. With AVC, you model the product once, capture the logic once, and reuse it endlessly. You can even define commonly sold configurations as material variants for stocking or quick quoting, while still relying on the same brains behind the scenes.

When You Should (Absolutely) Use Advanced Variant Configuration

Use AVC when your products are like LEGO sets—modular, customizable, and prone to enthusiastic customers wanting “just one more piece.” If your sales process involves choosing from a menu of features, if your master data threatens to spread like ivy, or if you need to introduce new options without rebuilding everything from scratch, AVC will save your roadmap (and your weekends). It’s especially handy in configure-to-order and engineer-to-order environments, where enforcing valid combinations isn’t a nice-to-have; it’s the difference between smooth production and emergency meetings.

Why Teams End Up Loving It

First, there’s the master data diet: one configurable material instead of thousands. Maintenance becomes manageable, and the risk of copy‑paste errors drops dramatically. Sales gets faster because the system helps them choose valid, buildable options without phoning a friend in engineering. Production gets exactly what was promised, complete with the right components and operations. And finance appreciates that configuration rules can gently steer choices toward cost‑appropriate combinations. In short, AVC keeps everyone aligned, which, in most companies, is rarer than a quiet inbox.

What You Need in Place

Success with AVC starts with a clear, honest picture of your product. That means well-defined characteristics that reflect how you actually sell and build, not how someone wished you did two reorgs ago. Your Super BOM and Super Routing should be comprehensive and well-structured, so dependencies can do their job cleanly. Each configurable material needs a configuration profile to control behavior, and your team should be comfortable with the Product Modeling Environment (PME/PMEVC) to maintain logic. Finally, make sure your modelers speak the language of dependencies—preconditions, selection conditions, procedures, and constraints—because that’s how your product rules come alive. If you’re moving to Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC), expect better performance and more modeling power with the same core principles—like upgrading from a trusty bike to an e‑bike that still pedals, but flies uphill.

The Payoff (and a Gentle Nudge)

Advanced Variant Configuration turns option overload into a controlled, elegant model. It keeps your master data slim, your configurations valid, and your lead times realistic. If your product line is already complex—or your roadmap suggests it soon will be—AVC is the practical way to offer customers choice without breeding chaos. A great way to start is to pick one product line, define the must-have characteristics, draft a Super BOM that mirrors reality, and pilot it with a few real orders. Iterate, tighten the rules, and watch the noise go down while speed and confidence go up.

 

If you’ve made it this far, congrats—you now speak fluent “configurable product.” The next best step is small and practical: pick one product line, sketch the must‑have characteristics, and let a pilot model prove the value. From there, you’ll refine the rules, trim the noise, and watch quoting and production get measurably smoother. May your dependencies be tidy, your BOMs be super, and your lead times politely short.

entry.title: Re: How you can manage Polish KSeF Requirement in SAP S/⁠4HANA Cloud Private Edition & SAP S/⁠4H
entry.content:
Hello. We use SAP ODN solution to store the KSEF IDs.

To create connection between existing FI document and KSEF ID ODN we do like this:


DATA: lfd_control_fields TYPE gslog_lfd_ctrl_fields,
            lfd_reference      TYPE if_gslog_local_fiscal_data=>tt_lfd_reference,

      mi_uf05a           TYPE uf05a,
      wa_bkpfx           TYPE ty_bkpfx,
      it_bkpfx           TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_bkpfx,
      mt_bkpf            TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF bkpf WITH DEFAULT KEY,
      mt_bkp1            TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF bkp1 WITH DEFAULT KEY,
      mt_bsec            TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF bsec WITH DEFAULT KEY,
      mt_bsed            TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF bsed WITH DEFAULT KEY,
      mt_bseg            TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF bseg WITH DEFAULT KEY,
      mt_bset            TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF bset WITH DEFAULT KEY,
      mt_bseu            TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF bseu WITH DEFAULT KEY,
      mi_bkdf            TYPE bkdf.

      SELECT * FROM bkpf INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE mt_bkpf
        WHERE bukrs = wa_bkpfx-bukrs
        AND   belnr = wa_bkpfx-belnr
        AND   gjahr = wa_bkpfx-gjahr.

      SELECT * FROM bseg
        INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE mt_bseg
        WHERE bukrs = wa_bkpfx-bukrs
        AND   belnr = wa_bkpfx-belnr
        AND gjahr = wa_bkpfx-gjahr.

      lfd_control_fields = VALUE #( document_type  = 'ADH'
                                    document_key_1 = wa_bkpfx-bukrs
                                    document_key_2 = wa_bkpfx-belnr
                                    document_key_3 = wa_bkpfx-gjahr
                                    record_type    = if_odn_constants=>lfd_record_type ).

      APPEND VALUE #( document_type  = 'ADH'
                      document_key_1 = wa_bkpfx-bukrs
                      document_key_2 = wa_bkpfx-belnr
                      document_key_3 = wa_bkpfx-gjahr
                      record_type    = if_odn_constants=>lfd_record_type
                      odn_type       = 'ZKSEF'
                      odn_value      = wa_bkpfx-odn
                      odn_country    = 'PL'
                      odn_int_type   = 'PLKSEF' ) TO lfd_reference.

      gslog_local_fiscal_data=>get_instance( )->set_entity( lfd = lfd_reference
                                                            lfd_control_fields = lfd_control_fields ).

      sy-tcode = 'FB60'.

      CALL FUNCTION 'GLO_ODN_FIN_BTE_00001030'
        EXPORTING
          i_bkdf        = mi_bkdf
          i_uf05a       = mi_uf05a
        TABLES
          t_bkp1        = mt_bkp1
          t_bkpf        = mt_bkpf
          t_bsec        = mt_bsec
          t_bsed        = mt_bsed
          t_bseg        = mt_bseg
          t_bset        = mt_bset
          t_bseu        = mt_bseu
        EXCEPTIONS
          error_message = 1.

      COMMIT WORK AND WAIT.

entry.title: Assistance class in WebDynpro ABAP
entry.content:
Introduction:

An Assistance Class is a special ABAP class that is allocated to a Web Dynpro component and offers reusable business logic, utility methods and data access capabilities not in the UI controller.
It assists in separation of the UI logic and business logic, as per the MVC principle.

Body:

  Generally in webdynpro ABAP we use to write all the coding part within the component itself but there is a provision with which we can write the necessary business logic outside the component that is in a class and we can use that class as the assistance class in the current component, Below are the steps involved in using this assistance class.

In the example We are creating a assistance class to get the business partner details.

Step1: Go to transaction se24 and create the class ZSAMP_ASSISTANCECLASS_BPDETAIL as shown below, this class will contain the logic to retrieve the partner details and it will be used as the assistance class in the webdynpro component

 

 

 

Step2: Create the method GET_PARTNER_DETAIL with the importing parameter as partner number and exporting parameter as partner details as shown below

 

 

Step3: Add the following code inside the method GET_PARTNER_DETAIL to retrieve the partner details

Method GET_PARTNER_DETAIL.
SELECT * FROM BUT000
INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE lt_but000
WHERE PARTNER = im_partner. 
Endmethod.

 

 

Step4: The class is ready, now create the WD component ZSAMP_ASSI and provide the class name as ZSAMP_ASSISTANCECLASS_BPDETAIL in the Assistance class field as indicated below

 



Step5:Also include the ALV component since the partner details will be displayed as ALV output, Create the view 'FIRST' with the input field element as partner number and after entering the partner number to search for the partner details include the button (search) element create the view elements as shown in the below screen

 



Step6: Make sure that the attribute WD_ASSIST is created in the FIRST view as shown below since this is the reference attribute of the assistance class which will be used to access the business logic of the class

 



Step7: After entering the partner number in the partner field the search button will be pressed so provide the following code in the event handler method of the search button to retrieve the partner details using the assistance class

method ONACTIONSEARCH .
data : lt_but000 TYPE TABLE OF but000.
data:
Elem_Context type ref to If_Wd_Context_Element,
Stru_Context type If_First=>Element_Context ,
Item_PARTNER like Stru_Context-PARTNER.
* get element via lead selection
Elem_Context = wd_Context->get_Element( ).
* get single attribute
Elem_Context->get_Attribute(
exporting
Name = `PARTNER`
importing
Value = Item_Partner ).
CALL METHOD WD_ASSIST->GET_PARTNER_DETAIL
EXPORTING
IM_PARTNER = Item_PARTNER
IMPORTING
LT_BUT000 = lt_but000
.
data: Node_Partnerdetail type ref to If_Wd_Context_Node,
      Elem_Partnerdetail type ref to If_Wd_Context_Element,
      Stru_Partnerdetail type If_First=>Element_Partnerdetail .
* navigate from <CONTEXT> to <PARTNERDETAIL> via lead selection
      Node_Partnerdetail = wd_Context->get_Child_Node( Name =        IF_FIRST=>wdctx_Partnerdetail ).
      Node_Partnerdetail->bind_table( lt_but000 ).
endmethod.

 

 

Step8: I made here to display the name details of the partner alone in the ALV output so restrict the fields in the context node of the FIRST view as shown below

 

 

Output:
Step9: The webynpro component using the Assistance class is created now and the output for the same will be displayed as follows


Conclusion: An Assistance Class in Web Dynpro ABAP helps separate business logic from UI logic, improving code modularity and maintainability. It enables reuse of functionality across components while aligning with the MVC design principle. Above are simple step by step process  how a Assistance class can be created and how to refer assistance class and methods in webdynpro abap code

entry.title: Re: How you can manage Polish KSeF Requirement in SAP S/⁠4HANA Cloud Private Edition & SAP S/⁠4H
entry.content:
@birgitlangerfeld 

To display ODN (KSEF ID) in FBL1N or FBL5N

Extend RFPOSX and RFPOSXEXT by KSEF ID data element

Create implementation of BAdI FI_ITEMS_CH_DATA

Implement method CHANGE_ITEMS

For vendor or customer line items (direct FI posting):

SELECT SINGLE odn_value FROM gslog_lfd
INTO <fs_items>-zksef
WHERE document_type = 'ADH'
AND document_key_1 = <fs_items>-bukrs
AND document_key_2 = <fs_items>-belnr
AND document_key_3 = <fs_items>-gjahr
AND odn_type = 'ZKSEF'.

For customer line item (from SD)

SELECT SINGLE odn_value FROM gslog_lfd
 INTO <fs_items>-zksef
  WHERE document_type = 'BDH'
  AND   document_key_1 = <fs_items>-xblnr
  AND   odn_type = 'ZKSEF'.

entry.title: Introducing the new SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library
entry.content:
The SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library has long been the go-to resource for anyone looking to plan and implement SAP Fiori applications. It provides a comprehensive catalog of all available apps, technical configuration details, installation requirements, and direct links to related resources like the Product Availability Matrix and Maintenance Planner.

We are now releasing a new version, rebuilt with a modern, intuitive interface that dramatically reduces the time it takes to find exactly what you need. More robust filtering lets you find adjacent apps so you can ensure an end-to-end SAP Fiori experience across your business processes.

Live now at fal.cloud.sap , built from the ground up with a focus on clarity and productivity, this new version transforms how SAP customers, functional consultants, partners, administrators and developers explore and implement SAP Fiori apps.

See it in action — watch an intro video

 

What's New - New Functionality That Improves Usability and Reduces App Search Time

New Home Page - A Smarter Starting Point That Gets You to Exactly What You're Looking For
On login, you are redirected to a new home page designed to get you to the right apps fast. A prominent search bar lets you query by keyword, while a Product Suite dropdown lets you scope results to SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP, or other environments before you even search.
The Quick Links section puts your most-used release versions one click away — from the latest editions to specific Feature Package Stacks like and Public Cloud releases. A side panel always keeps Home, Apps, Current Selections, and Saved Selections within reach.

 

A smarter starting point — find, filter, and navigate to your apps instantly
Search Wide, Drill Deep — New Filtering Gets You to the Right Apps Fast

In the third quarter of 2024, we released the new List View for the Fiori Apps Library — and the response has been overwhelmingly positive, with great feedback continuing to come in.

The enhanced Apps List View provides a streamlined interface for searching, filtering, and personalizing the display of available apps by product suite and release version. Use this feature to efficiently find, organize, and customize app information to meet your needs.

By default, the List View displays all available apps at their highest release version. Once you select a Product Suite and Release Version, the list instantly narrows to show only what is available in your environment. The horizontal filters at the top address your primary constraints — keyword search, product suite, and release version — for quick and easy navigation.

For more granular exploration, the vertical filter sidebar on the left enables faceted navigation, allowing you to drill down by UI Technology (Fiori, SAP GUI, SAPUI5, WebDynpro), Line of Business (Finance, Supply Chain, Sourcing, etc.), Role, and more.

Start broad, drill deep — layered filters surface the right apps
The two complementary filter layers that work in harmony to help you zero in on exactly the right apps. The horizontal filter bar lets you set your broad selection criteria — such as Product Suite, Release Version, or Line of Business — instantly scoping the entire library to your context (for example, filtering by Finance surfaces 4,898 relevant apps). The vertical facet panel then lets you refine further within those results — revealing that 1,014 of those Finance apps also belong specifically to Budget and Finance — without ever losing sight of the broader picture. Together, these two filter levels give you a powerful, layered approach to app discovery: start wide, drill deep, and arrive at a precise, meaningful shortlist in just a few clicks.

All the App Details You Need, Organised So You Can Find Them Without the Effort

The Detail View provides comprehensive information about an app — including its role, product suite, release details, app ID, features, and related apps — organized across several tabs. These cover important SAP Notes, installation requirements (front-end and back-end components), configuration requirements (SAPUI5 applications, OData services, and Fiori Launchpad data), business catalogs, groups, spaces, pages, business roles, as well as extensibility and support information. Together, these details help you assess an app's suitability and plan its implementation effectively.

Everything you need to assess and implement an app — now clearly organized

An Enhanced Selection Experience That Remembers What You're Working On — Every Time

The App Selection feature introduces a clear separation between app discovery and selection, while automatically saving your choices and preserving context throughout your session. It allows you to curate a specific list of applications for implementation, planning, or mass configuration — with selections carried over automatically across user sessions.

You can manage selections individually by using the toggle switch in the Selected column of the apps list view table, or in bulk using the Select All or Deselect All buttons at the top right of the table header. To focus exclusively on your chosen applications, click the Selected (n) tab to filter out all non-selected items.

Selection toggle for app

Current Selections, Always at Hand

Current Selection accessible from side bar, shows a consolidated view of all the apps you have selected across various places within the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library — for example, while browsing the apps list or search results. The key functionality here is the ability to filter this accumulated selection by Product Suite and Release Version, which lets you quickly check whether your chosen apps are available or not available in a specific release.

 

Current Selection shows all your chosen apps — filter by suite and release to confirm availability instantly
Saved Selections - Save Once, Reuse Anytime

Saved Selections also accessible from side bar, allows users to save their current selection under a named list for future reference, making it easy to revisit, load, or share curated sets of apps without having to re-select them each time. A particularly powerful feature visible here is the "Upload App Selection" option in the top right corner, which allows users to import a list of apps directly from a spreadsheet file.

 

Saved selections let you revisit, load, or share curated app sets across sessions

 

Work in the Language You're Most Comfortable In — Across 10 UI and 40 Content Languages

The library is available in 10 UI languages, configurable via the app or browser settings, with all search and filter categories — including roles, catalogs, and system objects — displayed in your selected language. For localized content, a separate Content Language setting supporting approximately 40 languages allows app metadata such as role names, business catalog titles, and app launcher subtitles to be displayed in your preferred language, making the library accessible and practical for global teams.

Choose your UI language and a separate content language for localized app metadata

 

What's Coming

Some features, such as Aggregate and Upgrade Analysis, are still making their way to the new version — in the meantime, they remain accessible via the classic library, with seamless navigation between the two. We are committed to continuously enhancing the new library, adding missing functionality and incorporating your feedback along the way.

Getting Started

Navigate to fal.cloud.sap and sign in using your SAP Universal ID, S-User, or P-User. Access to the SAP Fiori Apps Library is restricted to users authenticated via a SAP  Universal ID. If you don't have a Universal ID yet, you can register directly from the start page. Once signed in, your saved selections, view variants, and personalization settings are automatically tied to your account.

Explore and share your feedback.

entry.title: Sneak Peek in to SAP Analytics Cloud release for Q2 2026
entry.content:
As we enter Q2 2026 we take a look at some of the exciting features coming your way with the QRC2 2026 release of SAP Analytics Cloud. Let's dive in!

Asymmetric Reporting

Want to create tables that show different time periods for different versions, such as actuals and forecast?

In this release of the SAP Analytics Cloud, add in for Microsoft Excel, we introduce the asymmetric filter function allows you to filter on multiple dimension and member combinations with a single filter, letting you display different combinations of dimensions and members in the same table. Key Capabilities include: 

  • Apply distinct visibility filters per structure member
  • Hide unwanted parent levels across an entire hierarchical dimension.
  • Define exactly which hierarchy levels are visible, with automatic expansion to the highest selected level
  • Show or hide total rows and columns per structure member
  • Choose specific members to display for flat dimensions and navigational attributes
  • Automatically remove members excluded by the calculation context

 

For further details check out this demo video.

 

Composite Versioning

In QRC2 2026 we now allow composite designers to manage up to 10 versions of a composite. Composite designers are now able to restore or revert to previous versions through the version history whenever needed. Noting that only one active version can be used in a story.

This new feature reduces disruption for story viewers by allowing quick recovery to a stable version of a composite and improves reliability and safety when updating composite artifacts.

 

Job Monitor: inclusion of Data Export API jobs

A new Data Export API tab has been introduced in the job monitor where you can now check the status of your data extraction and delta calculation jobs. This valuable addition provides enhanced visibility of your API jobs that use the Data Export Service.

 

 

Data Panel

In this quarter we are excited to introduce the decoupled Data Panel, which can be accessed independently of the Builder Panel and Styling Panel. Now users will have a simplified right-hand toolbar for quick access to the Data Panel, Builder Panel, and Styling Panel. Key capabilities include:

  • Dynamic updates to the Data Panel based on the selected visualization and its underlying data model
  • Overview of all data models used within the story
  • Guided experience to add data from models, datasets, data sources, or files—with the flexibility to add data at any time
  • Quick navigation to the modeler and data analyzer directly from the Data Panel
  • Drag-and-drop support from the Data Panel to the Builder Panel or directly into visualizations

 

 

 

Recent and favorites in open and save dialogs

With this release users now can access Recent and Favorites directly within file open/save dialogs. These tabs allow quick access to recently used or starred stories, models, and folders, removing the need to navigate the file repository hierarchy. This feature works across stories, data actions, and other workflows. Key capabilities include:

  • Recent View: Displays a list of files recently accessed (created, edited, or opened).
  • Favorites View: Shows folders or files you have marked as a favorite, facilitating faster access.
  • Save Dialogs: Enables saving items directly into designated Favorite folders.

The Recent and Favorites views can be utilized in the Files repository and when using the Browse function to open/save content.

There are plenty more exciting deliverables coming your way this quarter and we will be taking a deeper dive into these items in our What’s New video and blog series so stay tuned. For a full list of all features coming in QRC2 2026 for SAP Analytics Cloud check out the Roadmap Explorer  and the What's New Viewer

Want to experience new and existing features for yourself? Take the leap and start your journey towards making data-driven decisions with confidence by signing up for a free trial, today.

entry.title: Re: IAS: loginName whitespace handling (lack of normaliza...
entry.content:
Hi @RACHID_AITBOUALE @MatthiasL,

and thanks for taking the time to look into the post.

Just to clarify a few points from my side:

  1. Source-system normalization
    Yes, for provisioning pipelines / IPS setups, data should be normalized by the source system before or during transformation. My initial post should have emphasized that tools like trim() exist for this and that my observation was primarily focused on the IAS UI, which can display values differently from how they are stored.

  2. Legacy system support
    Supporting legacy systems makes sense, even though I would assume this mostly applies to intermediate whitespaces. I’m curious under what circumstances trailing or leading whitespaces are actually used/required, especially multiple.

  3. Responsibility of data owners
    Every data owner should handle data consistently and provide normalized/sanitized values before pushing/pulling user-data to IAM components. I share this perspective.

  4. UI vs. backend behavior
    What really surprised me was that multiple leading or trailing whitespaces can be stored but disappear in view-mode. Even multiple intermediate whitespaces can be used, but these are reduced to a single whitespace: so setting "   user   name   "  becomes "user name" in non-edit-mode.

This means that inspecting IAS UI alone (outside of edit mode) doesn’t reveal the actual stored value. In SSO/IDP setups, this could result in “username looks correct” while the backend value is different, potentially causing subtle authentication mismatches.

I’ll look into submitting an Influence Request, as I haven’t used that process before.

Thank you again for your insights—they help contextualize the observation from both legacy support and provisioning perspectives.

Takeaway:
Even with source-system normalization, the IAS UI may hide critical whitespace differences, which could affect SSO/IDP consistency.

OnTop_BaseSalary_RO OnTop_BaseSalary_SA_GR OTBS_D_IT OnTop_BaseSal OnTop_BaseSalary_DH_GR OnTop_BaseSalary_Director_ES OnTop_BaseSalary_No_Dir_FR OnTop_BaseSalary_Operario_ES OnTop_BaseSalary_Q_IM_OP_IT OnTop_BaseSalary_SE OnTop_BaseSalary_Tariff_DE

SAP SuccessFactors Compensation

entry.title: Re: Datashpere Migration to S4HANA
entry.content:
Hi, I would not change the SAC Stories, nor the consumption AMs. I would keep the ECC PY data persisted in a table (disk) and Union it with the new S/4 data. 

For the "migration" ECC to S/4 I would use the compatibility views, SAP provided for exactly this purpose. So that one does not need to change the logic of the extractors. 

So search for compatibility views. Add them as source. Union with existing views and keep using the existing reporting without disturbance. 

Cheers, Martin. 

Here some links: 

https://me.sap.com/notes/2408083

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/some-interesting-facts-of-compatibility-views-in-sap-bw-4hana-and-sap-s/ba-p/13542675

entry.title: Re: EXCEPTION SEGMENT UNKNOWN RAISED error while calling RFC IDOCTYPE_READ_COMPLETE in Biztalk Middl
entry.content:
For now we wanted to solve this error and check if SAP S4 is actually feasible with 701 segment release and continue with Biztalk.

SAP S4 Privated Cloud 2023  ⇌ MS Biztalk 2016 ⇌ EDI Partners

Am worried and concerned "what if this error is solved and live in production but after few weeks it rans into outdated function issues either in Biztalk or SAP S4 that is extra risk it can be inherent and residual too.
